seams.trust_assumption: An onboarding and risk-decisioning system trusts that an identity exhibiting a consistent, aged, cross-source footprint (long-lived email, social graph, credit-bureau thin-file history, prior low-risk transactions, matching data-broker records) corresponds to a real natural person, treating accumulated correlated history as proof of genuine existence rather than as a fabricable artifact.
seams.violation: An attacker assembles and ages a fully synthetic identity graph (AI-generated persona, seeded social/email presence, manufactured thin-file credit history, planted data-broker entries) so the onboarding system's existence and risk checks all corroborate one another, and the fabricated person is admitted as genuine because every correlated signal the system trusts was itself author-controlled and patiently aged.

techniques.name: Aged synthetic-identity graph defeating cross-source existence checks
Manufactured thin-file/credit-bureau history bootstrapping a fabricated person

seams.rationale: Synthetic identity has been a fraud problem for years, but 2025-2027 changes the economics: generative AI can mass-produce coherent personas (faces, writing style, social activity) and agentic automation can seed and age the corroborating footprint across email, social, and data-broker surfaces at scale, so the cross-source consistency that risk engines treat as existence-proof becomes cheap to fabricate. The seam is that the identity layer infers authority/legitimacy from correlated history that the attacker authored, and there is no shared mechanism to attest that a footprint reflects a real natural person rather than a patiently-aged fabrication. Detection of synthetic graphs is only nascent and no tooling reconstructs the provenance of an identity's history, making this identity-authority seam novel at AI scale.
seams.suggested_research: Develop graph-provenance scoring that weights the independence and unforgeable-anchor strength of identity signals (e.g., longitudinal device/biometric anchors) over easily-seeded broker/social presence; build a synthetic-cohort detector that finds aged personas sharing a common fabrication fingerprint across institutions.

validation.validation_method: Construct a synthetic identity with an aged cross-source footprint (long-lived email, seeded social graph, manufactured thin-file credit history, planted data-broker entries) and submit it to an onboarding/KYC risk-decisioning system that scores existence via cross-source corroboration. Observe whether the fabricated person clears existence and risk checks because the correlated signals were all attacker-seeded.
validation.falsifier: Onboarding requires a liveness/biometric + authoritative government/issuer verification that the synthetic graph cannot satisfy, so cross-source footprint alone does not admit the identity.
validation.prerequisites: Time and resources to seed and age the synthetic graph; targeting systems that weight aged cross-source correlation as existence proof. Synthetic identity fraud is a well-documented, large-scale real-world phenomenon.
validation.existing_controls: Document + liveness biometric verification, authoritative-source (SSA eCBSV) checks, synthetic-identity fraud models, velocity/correlation-anomaly detection. Detection tooling exists in the fraud industry but is imperfect against patiently aged graphs.

seams.trust_assumption: An authorization plane trusts that every non-human identity it honors (service account, API key, OAuth app, CI token, agent credential, workload identity) maps to a currently-owned, actively-governed workload with an accountable human owner, so it keeps granting that credential's standing entitlements indefinitely because non-human identities have no login cadence, expiry pressure, or joiner-mover-leaver lifecycle to trigger review.
seams.violation: An attacker takes over or quietly reuses an orphaned NHI whose owning workload, project, or employee is long gone but whose credential and broad entitlements were never revoked, and the authorization plane honors it as a legitimate trusted workload because nothing ever questioned a non-human identity that simply kept working, granting persistent privileged access with no owner to notice.

techniques.name: Orphaned service-account / API-key reuse with intact standing entitlements
Ungoverned agent/workload identity sprawl outliving its workload

seams.rationale: Non-human identities already outnumber humans by large multiples and the 2025-2027 explosion of agent and workload credentials accelerates sprawl, yet IAM lifecycle governance (JML, recert, expiry) was built around human accounts that log in, change roles, and leave. NHIs have none of those natural review triggers, so orphaned service accounts and agent credentials with broad standing entitlements persist indefinitely with no owner watching, becoming durable trusted footholds. The seam is the authorization plane's assumption of continuous ownership behind every NHI; tooling to discover, attribute ownership, and right-size or expire NHIs is only nascent, and detection of an orphaned-credential reuse is weak because the identity 'belongs' there. This identity-sprawl seam is distinct from workload-federation misbinding because the failure is lifecycle abandonment, not trust-relationship configuration.
seams.suggested_research: Build an NHI lifecycle engine that binds every non-human credential to a live workload heartbeat and an accountable human owner, auto-expiring or quarantining credentials whose workload/owner disappears; measure entitlement-decay and orphan rates across a cloud estate and demonstrate silent reuse of an abandoned CI token.

validation.validation_method: Inventory non-human identities (service accounts, API keys, OAuth apps, CI/agent tokens) in a cloud tenant and correlate each to a live owning workload and an accountable human owner. Identify a credential whose workload/project/employee is gone but whose token still authenticates and retains standing entitlements; use it to perform a privileged action. Real if an orphaned NHI still authorizes privileged access with no owner/lifecycle to trigger revocation.
validation.falsifier: NHIs have enforced expiry/rotation, last-used-based auto-disable, and owner-attestation lifecycle (CIEM/NHI governance) so orphaned credentials are revoked before they can be reused, or no orphaned-with-entitlements credential is found.
validation.prerequisites: Discovery or possession of an orphaned credential (leaked key, abandoned SA) and an authorization plane with no expiry/last-used revocation on NHIs.
validation.existing_controls: NHI governance / CIEM tooling (Entra Workload ID, GCP SA key expiry & recommender, AWS IAM Access Analyzer / unused-access, NHI startups), key rotation, and last-used disable. Orphaned-credential abuse is a demonstrated real-world incident class (leaked/stale keys). Tooling and detection exist but adoption is partial.

seams.trust_assumption: An assistant/agent platform trusts that secrets and sensitive data pasted into a long context window, persistent conversation, or agent memory are ephemeral working state that lives only for the task, so it freely retains, logs, syncs, and exports chat history and memory without treating those stores as a credential vault subject to secret-scanning, redaction, or rotation policy.
seams.violation: Secrets (API keys, tokens, PII, internal URLs) that a user or tool dropped into a long-lived context window or agent memory are persisted into transcripts, vendor logs, training-feedback stores, or exportable chat history, and an attacker or over-broad integration later reads them from those secondary stores long after the task, because the platform never treated conversational/memory state as a durable secret-bearing data store.

techniques.name: Secret regurgitation from persisted long-context / agent memory
Chat-history / transcript export exposing retained credentials

seams.rationale: Million-token context windows and persistent agent memory in 2025-2027 mean users and tools routinely place secrets and PII into conversational state that the platform treats as transient, but that state is in fact persisted into transcripts, observability logs, feedback datasets, and exportable history that live far beyond the task and are reachable by integrations, support staff, or attackers. The seam is a time/state mismatch: the data is assumed ephemeral but is durably retained, and unlike the known prompt-cache timing leak this concerns long-lived storage of the cleartext itself, not a side channel. No platform runs secret-scanning/redaction over context and memory the way it would over a code repo, secrets in transcripts are never rotated, and detection of credential persistence in conversational stores is only nascent.
seams.suggested_research: Build a context/memory secret-scanner that detects and redacts credentials and PII before persistence, transcript export, or feedback capture, and triggers rotation on detected secrets; quantify secret-leak rates into transcript and observability stores across major assistant platforms.

validation.validation_method: Paste a unique canary secret (e.g., a tagged fake API key) into a long-context conversation or agent memory on an assistant platform. Action: trace where it lands — transcript exports, vendor request logs, feedback/training stores, synced chat history, integration-readable memory. Then, from a secondary store (export feature, log access, over-broad integration), attempt to read the canary back after the task ends. Observe whether the secret is recoverable from a secondary store and whether secret-scanning/redaction ever fired.
validation.falsifier: The platform secret-scans and redacts conversational/memory state before persistence/export, scopes memory so secondary stores cannot be read by other principals, and applies retention/rotation — so the canary is not recoverable from any secondary store after the task.
validation.prerequisites: Ability to place a secret into context/memory and later access a secondary store (export, logs, or an integration with memory read scope). Persistence of chat/memory to logs and exports is the common default behavior.
validation.existing_controls: Secret-scanning and redaction of conversations, memory access scoping, retention limits and disabling training on data, DLP on exports. This is essentially secrets-in-logs/secrets-in-history — a well-known data-handling problem; many platforms now redact, but coverage of memory/long-context as a secret store is inconsistent. Detection (secret scanning) is partial.

seams.trust_assumption: An audit / accountability layer trusts that the actor field on a logged action faithfully attributes that action to the principal who authorized it, so when an agent performs a tool call or API write the resulting log entry is treated as non-repudiable evidence of who decided to do it, even though the entry is just an assertion with no signed binding among the human authorizer, the agent identity, and the specific action.
seams.violation: Because no cryptographic provenance binds the authorizing human, the acting agent, and the exact action together, an action taken by a compromised or prompt-injected agent (or replayed/forged after the fact) is logged with a plausible actor and is indistinguishable from a legitimately-authorized one, so the accountability layer attributes it to an innocent principal and the true cause is unrecoverable and deniable.

techniques.name: Unsigned agent action lacking authorizer-to-action provenance binding
Forged/replayed actor attribution in agent audit trail

seams.rationale: As agents perform consequential writes across cloud, identity, and SaaS in 2025-2027, organizations rely on audit logs for accountability, but those logs record an unsigned actor assertion rather than a verifiable chain linking the human who approved a goal, the agent that executed it, and the precise action emitted. There is no provenance primitive that makes an agent action non-repudiable, so a compromised or injected agent's actions are forensically indistinguishable from authorized ones, blame lands on the wrong principal, and incident response cannot reconstruct true causation. This provenance seam is distinct from sub-agent context inheritance (which concerns authority flow) because it concerns after-the-fact attribution integrity; no agent framework signs an authorizer->agent->action triple and no detection flags missing or forged provenance.
seams.suggested_research: Define a signed action-provenance token that cryptographically binds the human authorization, the agent identity, the tool/parameters, and a nonce, emitted with every consequential action and verifiable by the audit layer; prototype a broker that rejects unprovenanced agent writes and demonstrate forensic attribution under a simulated agent compromise.

validation.validation_method: Instrument an agent that performs tool calls / API writes and inspect the resulting audit log entries. Attempt to (a) perform an action via a prompt-injected path and (b) forge/replay a log entry's actor field. Confirm there is no cryptographic binding among the human authorizer, agent identity, and the specific action — i.e., the log is an unsigned assertion attributable to an innocent principal.
validation.falsifier: The action pipeline produces a signed, tamper-evident provenance binding (e.g., signed action tokens, workload identity + request signing, transparency log) that ties authorizer-agent-action together and would distinguish a forged/replayed action.
validation.prerequisites: Access to an agent's action/audit pipeline; ability to inspect log format and attempt injected/forged actions.
validation.existing_controls: Most current agent stacks log unsigned actor strings; cryptographic action provenance (signed audit, SPIFFE workload identity, request signing) exists but is rarely applied end-to-end for agent actions. The gap (absence of binding) is real and largely unaddressed today.

seams.trust_assumption: An identity-decisioning or risk pipeline trusts that third-party data-broker and enrichment-API responses (firmographic lookups, email-reputation, device/IP intelligence, sanction/PEP screening, fraud-score augmentation) are authoritative external facts about a subject, so it feeds them directly into automated approve/deny/escalate decisions without treating the enrichment provider as an attacker-influenceable input.
seams.violation: An attacker poisons the upstream enrichment source (seeds false broker records, manipulates a reputation/scoring feed, or registers misleading device/IP attributes) so the enrichment API returns attacker-favorable 'facts,' and the identity pipeline ingests them as authoritative truth and makes the wrong trust decision, converting a data-feed manipulation into an identity-authority outcome the pipeline cannot trace back to the poisoned source.

techniques.name: Data-broker record seeding to bias an enrichment-API verdict
Reputation/risk-feed poisoning feeding automated identity decisions

seams.rationale: Identity and fraud pipelines in 2025-2027 increasingly outsource ground truth to data-broker and enrichment APIs and let agents auto-consume those verdicts, but the enrichment supply chain is loosely governed and partly attacker-influenceable (seeded broker entries, manipulable reputation feeds, declared device attributes), so a manipulated upstream fact silently steers a downstream authority decision. The seam is the pipeline's treatment of enrichment output as authoritative rather than as untrusted attacker-reachable data; this is a data-to-control violation on the identity plane distinct from synthetic-identity aging because here the attacker targets the institution's external truth source rather than building a persona. No pipeline carries provenance/confidence on enrichment facts, cross-validates conflicting providers, or detects feed poisoning, leaving this seam untooled and undetected.
seams.suggested_research: Build an enrichment-provenance layer that attaches source, freshness, and corroboration confidence to every third-party fact and requires multi-provider agreement before it can move an identity decision past a risk threshold; demonstrate broker-record seeding that flips an automated KYC/fraud verdict and measure detectability.

validation.validation_method: In an identity/risk pipeline that ingests a data-broker or reputation/IP-intel feed, seed attacker-favorable records upstream (create false firmographic/broker entries, age an IP/domain to gain reputation, register misleading device attributes) and submit a subject that triggers the enrichment lookup. Observe whether the poisoned 'facts' flip an automated approve/deny/escalate decision. Seam confirmed if the manipulated feed changes the decision and the pipeline cannot trace the poisoned source.
validation.falsifier: The pipeline corroborates enrichment against independent signals or treats single-source enrichment as advisory only (human review, multi-source consensus), so a single poisoned feed cannot move the decision.
validation.prerequisites: Ability to influence the upstream broker/reputation source (record seeding, reputation aging, registration), and a pipeline that auto-decisions on the feed.
validation.existing_controls: Multi-source corroboration, provider data-quality/fraud controls, manual review thresholds, anomaly checks on enrichment values. IP/reputation aging and broker-record manipulation are demonstrated in adjacent fraud contexts.

seams.trust_assumption: A relying party that has been provisioned a hybrid post-quantum certificate or hybrid key-exchange group (classical+ML-KEM/ML-DSA) trusts that because the peer is PQC-capable, the negotiated session inherited the quantum-resistant half of the hybrid, so it records the connection as 'PQC-protected' in its posture and compliance state even when only the classical component actually established the shared secret.
seams.violation: An on-path attacker strips or fails the PQC half of the hybrid (drops the ML-KEM keyshare, forces a classical-only group, or presents the classical-only fallback chain a dual-stack deployment still accepts for legacy reach), and the relying party silently completes a purely classical session while its telemetry, audit log, and crypto-agility dashboard still report a quantum-safe handshake — leaving a harvestable session mislabeled as protected.

techniques.name: Hybrid keyshare strip forcing classical-only PQC fallback
PQC posture mislabeling of a downgraded hybrid session

seams.rationale: The 2025-2027 migration wave deploys hybrid certs and hybrid key exchange in dual-stack mode precisely so that PQC-incapable peers still connect, which means a classical-only outcome is a designed-in success, not an error. The dangerous seam is not the downgrade itself but that the relying party's posture/compliance accounting credits the connection as quantum-safe based on the cert's hybrid *capability* rather than the session's actual negotiated *outcome*, so an attacker who forces the classical path also forges the organization's record of being PQC-protected. This is distinct from generic TLS downgrade because the violated trust is the PQC-readiness bookkeeping layer that migration programs rely on to declare risk retired; no scanner today reconciles 'hybrid cert present' against 'PQC component actually used per session', and no IDS flags a hybrid-capable peer that settled on classical.
seams.suggested_research: Build a session-level reconciler that parses negotiated KEM groups and signature algorithms from live handshakes and diffs them against the hybrid capability advertised in the served certificate, alerting when a PQC-capable endpoint completes classical-only; red-team a dual-stack hybrid deployment by selectively dropping ML-KEM keyshares and confirming the compliance dashboard still reports quantum-safe.

validation.validation_method: Set up a hybrid PQC-capable client/server (classical+ML-KEM, e.g., X25519MLKEM768) plus a posture/compliance logger that records the connection as 'PQC-protected'. As an on-path attacker, strip the hybrid group from the ClientHello supported_groups (or force the classical-only fallback the dual-stack deployment still accepts). Observe whether the session completes classical-only while the telemetry/audit still labels it quantum-safe.
validation.falsifier: The endpoints refuse to complete (or the posture logger flags) any session that did not negotiate the PQC group — i.e., hybrid is mandatory and downgrade causes handshake failure, not a silently-mislabeled classical session.
validation.prerequisites: On-path position to modify the handshake; a dual-stack deployment that still accepts classical-only for legacy reach; posture logging that infers PQC from capability rather than negotiated group.
validation.existing_controls: TLS downgrade protections cover classical version/cipher rollback; mandating the hybrid group (fail-closed) and logging the actually-negotiated group rather than capability. Note: hybrid KEX itself resists downgrade if hybrid is required, so the seam depends on a permissive fallback config.

seams.trust_assumption: A crypto-agility control plane (centralized cipher-policy service, KMS algorithm profile, or fleet crypto-policy CRD) is trusted by every workload that reads it to only ratchet algorithm strength forward, so workloads cache and apply the distributed policy without re-validating that a newly fetched 'allowed algorithms' set is not weaker than the one they were already enforcing.
seams.violation: An attacker with write access to the agility policy store (or who replays an older signed policy version) pushes a policy revision that re-enables a deprecated suite or lowers the minimum negotiated group, and the fleet's workloads apply it as an authoritative forward update because the agility mechanism designed to make algorithms changeable has no monotonic floor — making the very lever built to strengthen crypto into a one-command fleet-wide weakening primitive.

techniques.name: Crypto-agility policy rollback to deprecated suite
Signed-policy replay reintroducing weak algorithm floor

seams.rationale: Crypto-agility is the headline answer to PQC migration: orgs are standing up central policy planes in 2025-2027 so they can swap algorithms without redeploying code. But agility is bidirectional by construction, and the deployment model is 'workloads trust the latest policy the plane hands them.' Nobody is treating the algorithm-strength dimension as a monotonic counter the way anti-rollback treats firmware versions, so a compromised or replayed policy revision quietly relaxes the entire fleet at once and looks like a normal config push. This is a new seam distinct from per-connection downgrade and from the excluded crypto-agility-rollback hint's framing of negotiation: here the trust violated is the distribution channel's implicit monotonicity, landing on every workload that treats centralized crypto policy as authoritative. No policy plane enforces a strength floor or signs a non-decreasing version, and SIEMs do not parse policy diffs for strength regressions.
seams.suggested_research: Prototype a monotonic-strength guard that ranks each pushed crypto policy on a partial order of algorithm strength and refuses or quarantines any revision below the highest previously-applied floor, with attestation that the floor counter is tamper-evident; demonstrate fleet weakening via a replayed older signed policy against a KMS-driven cipher profile.

validation.validation_method: In a fleet that fetches an 'allowed algorithms' policy from a central store (KMS profile, crypto-policy CRD, config service), push or replay an older signed policy revision that re-enables a deprecated suite or lowers the minimum group. Observe whether workloads apply it as a forward update without checking monotonicity. Real if the fleet negotiates the weaker suite after the rollback because no monotonic floor is enforced.
validation.falsifier: Workloads enforce a hard-coded local minimum floor, reject policy versions lower than currently enforced (monotonic version + strength check), or signed policies carry non-decreasing version with anti-rollback (counter/epoch) verification, so a weaker/replayed policy is refused.
validation.prerequisites: Write access to the agility policy store or ability to replay a validly-signed older revision, and clients that apply policy without a monotonic-strength floor or anti-rollback check.
validation.existing_controls: Signed policy with version/rollback protection, local minimum-strength floors, and config-store RBAC. Anti-rollback for signed artifacts is a known pattern (firmware), but crypto-agility-specific rollback floors are not standardized. Largely a design concern; no public PoC of this specific seam.

seams.trust_assumption: An organization that enables TLS session-key export (SSLKEYLOGFILE-style decryption for observability, WAF, DLP, or eBPF middleboxes) trusts that the exported per-session keys are ephemeral operational artifacts that live only as long as the flow inspection needs them, so it pipes them into the same log/observability and long-term storage pipeline as ordinary telemetry without a separate confidentiality lifecycle.
seams.violation: The exported session keys are aggregated into centralized observability/SIEM and cold archival storage alongside passively-captured ciphertext flows, so an attacker (or a future quantum adversary) who reaches the archive obtains both the long-lived stored ciphertext and the very keys to decrypt it — collapsing the harvest-now-decrypt-later defense organizations think PQC migration gives them, because the plaintext-equivalent keys were retained next to the data the whole time.

techniques.name: Exported TLS session keys co-archived with captured ciphertext
Observability keylog pipeline as long-lived decryption oracle

seams.rationale: Enterprises increasingly export TLS keys to inspect their own encrypted traffic (eBPF observability, cloud-native WAFs, DLP), and separately are migrating to PQC to defend against harvest-now-decrypt-later. The overlooked seam is that the keylog export pipeline and the packet/flow capture pipeline both feed the same SIEM and the same multi-year cold storage, so an org that believes PQC has retired its HNDL exposure has actually been co-locating plaintext-equivalent keys with the ciphertext in long-lived storage — no quantum computer required, just archive access. This is distinct from the passive-capture HNDL hint because the violated trust is the assumption that exported keys are ephemeral; the danger is the archival co-location bridge, which no DLP/observability product treats as a confidentiality boundary and no retention policy isolates. There is no tooling that detects session keys and ciphertext sharing a retention tier and no detection for keylog material leaking into long-term stores.
seams.suggested_research: Map real observability/WAF stacks for where SSLKEYLOG-equivalent material lands and whether it shares a retention class with captured flows; build a storage-classifier that flags any tier holding both decryption keys and corresponding ciphertext, and propose an ephemeral key-escrow with enforced short TTL distinct from the telemetry lake.

validation.validation_method: In a lab, enable SSLKEYLOGFILE-style session-key export for observability/DLP. Confirm whether the exported per-session keys flow into the same SIEM/observability and cold-archive pipeline as captured ciphertext PCAPs. Action: as someone with archive read access, retrieve both an archived ciphertext flow and its co-stored session keys and decrypt the flow offline. Observe whether the archive co-locates plaintext-equivalent keys with the ciphertext they unlock.
validation.falsifier: Exported session keys are held only ephemerally for live inspection and are never written to the same long-term archive as ciphertext (separate confidentiality lifecycle / short retention / separate access domain) — so an archive reader cannot obtain both ciphertext and keys.
validation.prerequisites: An org that exports TLS session keys for inspection AND pipes them into the same logging/archive as captured flows, plus read access to that archive. Whether keys are co-archived is an operational/config question; many deployments keep keys ephemeral.
validation.existing_controls: Ephemeral key handling, separate key store with short retention distinct from flow archives, not archiving raw ciphertext flows long-term, key access segregation. The harvest-now-decrypt-later concern is well known; co-archiving keys with ciphertext is a deployment anti-pattern but not a guaranteed default — depends heavily on config.

seams.trust_assumption: An edge-compute platform (V8-isolate Workers, functions-at-edge) trusts that the per-request isolate it hands a tenant's code starts from a clean, request-scoped global state, so tenant code freely caches secrets, decrypted tokens, and per-user context in module-level globals on the assumption that reuse only ever serves the same tenant's subsequent requests on the same warmed isolate.
seams.violation: The platform's isolate-reuse / cross-request global persistence (warm isolates kept alive across requests, or a module-scope variable surviving between invocations) is observed by a co-resident or follow-on request from a different tenant or user routed to the same warmed isolate, so module-level secrets and decrypted context from request N leak into the execution context of request N+1 that the tenant never intended to share — a serverless analog of cold-start bleed but at the isolate-global granularity edge platforms uniquely expose.

techniques.name: Edge isolate module-global secret bleed across requests
Warm-isolate context inheritance across tenants

seams.rationale: Edge platforms aggressively reuse warm V8 isolates and reused execution contexts to kill cold-start latency, and their developer guidance does warn against global state — but in 2025-2027 the explosion of edge code that decrypts tokens, holds KV-fetched secrets, and caches per-user auth context in module globals turns that performance optimization into a confidentiality seam. The trust violated is context-scoping: developers and the platform implicitly assume request isolation, while the isolate model actually grants request N+1 inherited access to whatever request N left in scope. This is a distinct gap from serverless cold-start bleed framed for VM/container functions because edge isolates share a single process and reuse globals at far finer granularity and far higher reuse rates, with no per-request scrubbing. There is no tooling that detects module-global secret retention across edge invocations and no platform-side or detection signal for cross-request context inheritance.
seams.suggested_research: Build a static/dynamic analyzer for edge Worker code that flags secrets or decrypted context persisting in module scope across the request boundary, plus a runtime probe that measures whether warmed isolates leak prior-request globals to a differently-keyed follow-on request; quantify reuse rates on a major edge platform.

validation.validation_method: Deploy code to an edge isolate platform (Cloudflare Workers / similar) that caches a secret or per-user token in a module-level global. From a second tenant/user request routed to a warmed isolate, read the module global and check for the prior request's secret. Confirm whether globals persist across requests within an isolate and whether cross-tenant code can ever co-reside in the same isolate.
validation.falsifier: The platform never co-locates different tenants' code in one isolate (each tenant gets isolated isolates) so module globals only ever serve the same tenant; the observed 'bleed' is within a single tenant's own requests (a known documented behavior), not cross-tenant.
validation.prerequisites: Ability to deploy code to the edge platform; understanding of its isolate-reuse and tenant-placement model.
validation.existing_controls: Cloudflare Workers documents that globals persist across requests for the SAME Worker and warns against storing per-request state there; tenants do not share isolates. Cross-tenant bleed would require a placement/isolation flaw not publicly demonstrated. Single-tenant cross-request global leakage is real but is a coding pitfall, not a cross-tenant seam.

seams.trust_assumption: A CDN/edge cache trusts that its computed cache key (the normalized subset of request attributes it hashes) fully captures every input the origin used to authorize and personalize a response, so a cache hit for a given key is safe to serve to any subsequent requester that produces the same key, regardless of headers, tenancy claims, or edge-function transforms not folded into the key.
seams.violation: An attacker crafts requests where an authorization- or tenant-determining input (an edge-function-injected header, a tenant token, a Vary-omitted field) influences the personalized/authorized origin response but is excluded from the cache key, so the edge caches a victim-scoped or differently-tenanted response and serves it cross-tenant to attacker requests — a confused-deputy disclosure born from the mismatch between what authorizes the response and what keys the cache.

techniques.name: Cache-key/authorization-input mismatch cross-tenant serve
Edge-function-injected header excluded from cache key

seams.rationale: Modern edge stacks let functions-at-edge mutate requests (inject identity headers, rewrite tenancy) and personalize responses before and after the cache, while the cache key is a separately-configured normalized hash. In 2025-2027 deployments the set of inputs that authorize a response has drifted away from the set folded into the cache key, especially when edge middleware adds tenant context the cache config never learned about. The violated trust is the cache's assumption that its key is a complete authorization fingerprint; the result is a confused deputy that serves one tenant's authorized content to another. This is broader and more current than classic web-cache-deception/poisoning because the divergence is now manufactured by programmable edge functions rather than just header quirks. Cache-key auditing tooling is only nascent and there is no detection that correlates an authorization-relevant input the origin honored against the keying config the edge actually used.
seams.suggested_research: Develop a differential probe that enumerates request attributes, classifies which ones change the authorized/personalized origin response, and diffs that set against the live cache-key and Vary configuration to surface unkeyed authorization inputs; demonstrate cross-tenant cache serve through an edge-function-injected tenant header excluded from the key.

validation.validation_method: Deploy a CDN/edge config (e.g. Cloudflare/Fastly/CloudFront) where the origin authorizes/personalizes a response using an input (a tenant token, an edge-function-injected header, a Vary-omitted field) that is NOT part of the computed cache key. As victim, populate the cache with a scoped response; as attacker, issue a request that produces the same cache key and observe whether the victim-scoped/cross-tenant response is served. Seam confirmed on cross-tenant disclosure. This is web cache deception / key-confusion.
validation.falsifier: All authorization/personalization inputs are folded into the cache key or Vary, or auth'd responses are marked private/no-store, so a different requester never gets a cache hit on someone else's response.
validation.prerequisites: A CDN config with a mismatch between authorizing inputs and the cache key (misconfigured Vary, header injection by edge functions, tenant token outside key). Attacker needs only normal request access.
validation.existing_controls: Correct Cache-Key/Vary configuration, Cache-Control private/no-store on authorized content, cache deception protections. Web cache deception and key-confusion disclosures are publicly demonstrated (multiple real incidents/bug bounties).

seams.trust_assumption: A workload that pulls short-lived credentials from a secrets manager (dynamic DB creds, leased cloud tokens) trusts that a credential it successfully cached is valid and authoritative for its full advertised TTL, and the issuing manager trusts that revoking/rotating the old secret at rotation time atomically retires it everywhere — so neither side accounts for the window in which a rotated-out credential is still accepted by a downstream resource that has not yet refreshed its trust material.
seams.violation: An attacker who captured a leased credential continues to use it during the rotation overlap window, because the secrets manager's rotation marks a new secret active but the downstream datastore/service still honors the prior one until its own cache or grace period expires, and the client meanwhile keeps presenting a cached lease past its real revocation — turning the rotation seam into a live re-use window where a credential is simultaneously 'rotated out' at the manager and 'still good' at the resource.

techniques.name: Rotated-out lease accepted during downstream grace overlap
Client cache honoring credential past real revocation

seams.rationale: Dynamic secrets and short-TTL leases are sold as the answer to credential theft, but the operational reality in 2025-2027 is that rotation is never atomic across the three parties: the manager (Vault/cloud secrets), the client cache, and the downstream resource that validates the credential each maintain independent notions of validity, and rotation deliberately overlaps old and new to avoid outages. The violated trust is the assumption that 'rotated' means 'no longer accepted'; the overlap window is a designed-in re-use opportunity that a captured credential exploits with no anomaly because both old and new are momentarily legitimate. This is distinct from generic credential theft and from the excluded workload-identity-federation gap because the seam is the temporal non-atomicity of revocation across a distributed trust set, not the federation binding. Detection is only nascent (some managers log lease use) and no tooling reconciles 'manager says rotated' against 'resource still accepts' to expose the live overlap.
seams.suggested_research: Instrument a Vault-or-cloud dynamic-secret pipeline to measure the real revocation-overlap window across manager, client cache, and downstream resource, and build a detector that flags use of a lease the manager already rotated but the resource still honors; prototype a rotation protocol that proves downstream retirement before marking a secret rotated.

validation.validation_method: Configure a secrets manager (e.g., Vault dynamic DB creds) issuing short-lived leases to a workload, with a downstream datastore that has its own credential cache/grace. Capture a leased credential, trigger rotation, and during the overlap window attempt to authenticate to the downstream resource with the rotated-out credential. Measure whether it is still honored after the manager marks it rotated.
validation.falsifier: Rotation atomically revokes the old credential at the resource (immediate invalidation, no grace), so the captured credential fails the moment rotation occurs — no usable overlap window.
validation.prerequisites: Possession of a leased credential and timing knowledge of rotation; a downstream resource with non-atomic/grace credential acceptance. Common in dynamic-secret + caching architectures.
validation.existing_controls: Atomic revocation at the resource, short overlap windows with active session termination, lease-bound source-IP/mTLS constraints, monitoring for use of rotated-out credentials. Rotation overlap is a recognized operational hazard.

seams.trust_assumption: An eUICC/eSIM consumer device trusts that an operational profile delivered through the GSMA remote-SIM-provisioning (RSP) flow — once the activation code / QR / carrier-app handoff completes — was authorized by the legitimate subscriber for the legitimate operator, so the modem installs the profile and binds the device's network identity to it without independently re-verifying who actually initiated the provisioning.
seams.violation: An attacker who hijacks the provisioning initiation (a phished activation code, a malicious carrier-app deep link, or an SM-DP+ address swap) drives the RSP flow to install an attacker-chosen profile or download a profile onto an attacker-controlled eUICC, so the subscriber's mobile network identity is silently bound to a profile the genuine subscriber never authorized — an eSIM-swap that needs no physical SIM and leaves the provenance of the provisioning request unverified at the device.

techniques.name: RSP activation-code/QR hijack installing unauthorized profile
SM-DP+ address swap redirecting profile download

seams.rationale: eSIM is becoming default on flagship phones and IoT in 2025-2027 (carrier-only US iPhones, eSIM-only IoT modules), and the RSP trust chain authenticates the SM-DP+ server and the eUICC chip cryptographically but does not bind the provisioning *initiation* to verified subscriber intent — the activation code or carrier-app handoff is the soft seam. The violated trust is provenance of the request: the device treats a completed RSP handshake as proof the right human asked for this profile, when an attacker who captures the initiation can swap identity without ever touching hardware, defeating the physical-possession assumption that made classic SIM-swap need an insider. This is a fresh, lightly-tooled telecom seam distinct from generic SIM-swap because it lives in the remote-provisioning protocol layer and the device-side initiation binding, where no consumer detection exists and no tooling verifies initiation provenance.
seams.suggested_research: Analyze the consumer RSP initiation path (activation codes, carrier-app deep links, default SM-DP+ resolution) for points where request provenance is unverified, and prototype a subscriber-bound initiation token plus a device-side prompt that surfaces the requesting operator identity and a fraud signal for out-of-band profile downloads; demonstrate profile redirection via a swapped SM-DP+ address.

validation.validation_method: Reproduce an eSIM-swap by hijacking the RSP initiation: phish/social-engineer the carrier into issuing an activation code to an attacker, or manipulate a carrier-app deep link / SM-DP+ activation handoff so an attacker-chosen profile downloads onto an attacker eUICC. Confirm the subscriber's network identity binds to the attacker device. Real if provisioning completes without the device/operator re-verifying the initiator's authority.
validation.falsifier: The RSP flow enforces strong subscriber authentication at initiation (operator-side MFA/identity proofing), eUICC matching-ID + confirmation-code binding, and operator fraud controls that detect anomalous profile downloads, so a hijacked initiation cannot complete.
validation.prerequisites: Ability to compromise the provisioning initiation (carrier social engineering, malicious carrier-app link, or SM-DP+ address manipulation). The device-side RSP protocol itself does not authenticate the human initiator; the control lives operator-side.
validation.existing_controls: GSMA SGP.22 confirmation codes / matching IDs, operator-side identity proofing and fraud detection, and carrier SIM-swap protections. eSIM-swap / SIM-swap fraud is a demonstrated real-world attack class, though it exploits operator process more than a device protocol flaw.

seams.trust_assumption: A 5G subscriber trusts that SUCI concealment (encrypting the SUPI/IMSI with the home network's public key before it ever leaves the device) makes their permanent identity unlinkable on the radio interface, so the privacy guarantee of the standard is treated as protecting against the IMSI-catcher tracking that plagued earlier generations.
seams.violation: An attacker correlates the side-channel and protocol residue that SUCI does not conceal — stable per-device parameters, fixed home-network routing identifiers, deterministic SUCI re-computation patterns under a misconfigured null-scheme or reused randomness, and timing/capability fingerprints — to re-link successive concealed identifiers back to one subscriber across sessions, defeating the unlinkability the SUCI design is trusted to provide without ever recovering the cleartext SUPI.

techniques.name: SUCI cross-session linkability via unconcealed routing/capability residue
Null-scheme / reused-randomness SUCI re-identification

seams.rationale: 5G SUCI is marketed as the cure for IMSI-catchers, and standalone 5G deployment ramps through 2025-2027, so operators and regulators increasingly treat the radio-layer identity-privacy box as checked. The seam is that SUCI conceals the SUPI but leaves a constellation of unconcealed or weakly-randomized fields (routing indicator, home-network PKI key id, device capability fingerprints, and — where operators ship the null scheme or weak randomness — recomputable concealed values) that an analytic adversary fuses to re-establish linkability across sessions. The violated trust is the unlinkability provenance of the concealed identifier itself. This is a current, untooled privacy gap distinct from classic IMSI-catching because the attack is correlation against the metadata the privacy scheme does not cover, not interception of cleartext. No tooling measures real-world SUCI linkability and no detection flags re-identification campaigns.
seams.suggested_research: Capture SUCI exchanges across a controlled 5G SA testbed and measure cross-session linkability from routing indicators, capability fingerprints, and randomness quality, including operator configurations that fall back to the null scheme; build a linkability-scoring tool operators can run to validate that their SUCI deployment actually delivers unlinkability.

validation.validation_method: Capture 5G NAS registration traffic across multiple sessions for a target device using an SDR-based 5G sniffer. Action: extract unconcealed residue (routing indicator, home network identifier, UE capability fingerprint, timing patterns; and test for null-scheme misconfiguration or reused randomness producing deterministic SUCI). Correlate these stable features across sessions to test whether successive concealed SUCIs can be re-linked to one subscriber without recovering the SUPI. Measure linkability accuracy vs a single-subscriber ground truth.
validation.falsifier: The unconcealed residue is not sufficiently device-stable or distinctive to re-link sessions above chance, and SUCI is computed with proper profile A/B (fresh ephemeral keys, non-null scheme) yielding non-deterministic ciphertext — so successive identifiers cannot be correlated to one subscriber.
validation.prerequisites: 5G radio capture capability (SDR + 5G NAS decoding) near the target and multiple observed registrations. Routing-indicator and capability-fingerprint linkability is studied in academic literature; null-scheme misconfig is a real but specific failure.
validation.existing_controls: Mandatory non-null SUCI encryption scheme, fresh ephemeral keys per computation, routing-indicator privacy considerations, capability-transfer protection in later releases. Linkability via residue is an emerging research finding; no productized detection.

seams.trust_assumption: A verifier of a keyless-signed OCI artifact (Sigstore/Fulcio short-lived cert tying a signature to an OIDC identity) trusts that the OIDC identity baked into the signing certificate corresponds to a legitimately-controlled, human- or pipeline-owned account, so an artifact whose transparency-log entry maps to a recognizable identity string is accepted as authentically published by that party.
seams.violation: An attacker who controls a phishable, typosquatted, dormant, or workflow-impersonating OIDC identity (a compromised CI workflow ref, a look-alike email/GitHub identity, a reused org account) obtains a valid Fulcio certificate and signs a malicious artifact, and the verifier's policy admits it because the identity *string* matches an allowed pattern even though the binding from that OIDC identity to a trustworthy real-world publisher was never itself validated — the keyless model moved the trust to an OIDC identity that the verifier never authenticated the provenance of.

techniques.name: Phishable/typosquatted OIDC identity yielding valid keyless signature
Workflow-ref impersonation in keyless cert identity claim

seams.rationale: Keyless signing (Sigstore/Fulcio/Rekor) is the fast-spreading 2025-2027 answer to 'no more long-lived signing keys,' and verifiers increasingly gate artifact admission on the OIDC identity embedded in the short-lived cert. The overlooked seam is that the security now rests entirely on the trustworthiness of that OIDC identity binding, which was itself phishable, typosquattable, or impersonable at the CI-workflow level — the verifier authenticates that *a* valid OIDC identity signed, not that the identity legitimately represents the publisher it claims. This is explicitly distinct from CA mis-issuance (no rogue CA needed; Fulcio behaves correctly) and from SLSA provenance scope: the violated trust is the identity-binding semantics of keyless signatures and the verifier's pattern-matching policy. Identity-allowlist tooling is only nascent and brittle to look-alikes, and there is no detection for keyless signatures from newly-seen, dormant, or impersonating identities.
seams.suggested_research: Build an identity-binding risk scorer for keyless-signature policies that flags look-alike, newly-registered, dormant, or workflow-ref-mismatched OIDC identities and requires reputation/age evidence before admission; demonstrate admitting a malicious artifact via a typosquatted OIDC identity that satisfies a naive identity-pattern verification policy.

validation.validation_method: Using Sigstore/Fulcio in a test setup, obtain a valid keyless signing cert for an OIDC identity an attacker controls (e.g., a look-alike GitHub account, a typosquatted email, or a compromised workflow ref) and sign a malicious OCI artifact. Configure a verifier with a pattern-matching identity policy and confirm it admits the artifact because the identity string matches the allowed pattern though the real-world publisher binding was never validated.
validation.falsifier: Verifier policy pins exact, attacker-uncontrollable identities (specific repo + workflow ref + issuer) such that a typosquatted/look-alike OIDC identity does not match, so the malicious artifact is rejected.
validation.prerequisites: Control of a plausible-looking OIDC identity; ability to publish an artifact and a verifier with loose identity-pattern policy.
validation.existing_controls: Sigstore docs stress pinning exact issuer+identity (e.g., specific GitHub workflow ref) precisely to avoid this; the seam is loose-policy or typosquat-trust misconfiguration. The class (signing != trustworthy publisher) is acknowledged in supply-chain security discourse.

seams.trust_assumption: A DeFi protocol that switched from spot price to a time-weighted average price (TWAP) oracle trusts that averaging over a window of recent observations makes the reported price economically infeasible to manipulate, so it treats the TWAP read at the moment of a liquidation, mint, or collateral revaluation as manipulation-resistant ground truth and sizes its safety margins accordingly.
seams.violation: An attacker who controls block production cadence (a proposer/builder via MEV markets, an L2 sequencer, or a low-liquidity pool on a chain with long or irregular block times) skews the observations that fall inside the TWAP window — stuffing or spacing blocks so a brief manipulated price dominates the time-weighting — and extracts value during the exact transaction that reads the average, defeating the window's averaging by warping the time dimension the average is computed over rather than the prices themselves.

techniques.name: Block-cadence warping of TWAP observation window
Sequencer-controlled timestamp skew of weighted price

seams.rationale: After the flash-loan spot-oracle disasters, the entire DeFi industry treated TWAP as the cure, and 2025-2027 protocols on app-chains, rollups, and alt-L1s deploy short-window TWAPs as their canonical manipulation-resistant feed. The overlooked seam is that TWAP's security rests on an assumption about *time* — that observations are sampled at honest, roughly-uniform intervals — yet on chains where the attacker influences block timing (centralized L2 sequencers, MEV proposer control, configurable block times) the weighting can be skewed without sustaining the manipulated price, so a brief excursion gains outsized weight. This is distinct from the classic flash-loan spot-oracle hint because the violated trust is the temporal honesty of the sampling, not the instantaneous price; it is a time/state primitive failure that becomes exploitable precisely on the new high-throughput, single-sequencer venues protocols are migrating to. No oracle library validates inter-observation timing integrity against the sequencer, and no monitor flags a liquidation whose TWAP window was dominated by anomalously-spaced blocks.
seams.suggested_research: Build a TWAP-window auditor that reconstructs the block-timestamp distribution feeding each oracle read and scores how concentrated the time-weight was on suspicious intervals, alerting on liquidations/mints whose window was cadence-skewed; on a single-sequencer L2 testbed, demonstrate value extraction by spacing blocks to over-weight a transient manipulated price without holding it.

validation.validation_method: On a testnet/fork, deploy a protocol using a short-window on-chain TWAP (e.g. Uniswap v2/v3 cumulative-price oracle) backing liquidation/mint. As an attacker controlling block production cadence (local sequencer, or a forked chain where you control block timestamps/ordering) on a low-liquidity pool, manipulate price and stuff/space blocks so the manipulated observations dominate the time-weighting, then trigger the dependent action within the warped window. Measure extracted value vs. a fair TWAP.
validation.falsifier: The TWAP window is long enough and the pool liquid enough that controlling cadence cannot make a brief manipulated price dominate, or the protocol cross-checks against an independent oracle (e.g. Chainlink) so the warped TWAP doesn't drive the action.
validation.prerequisites: Control of block production cadence (own L2 sequencer, proposer/builder power, or a chain with long/irregular block times) and a low-liquidity pool; a protocol relying on a short on-chain TWAP.
validation.existing_controls: Longer TWAP windows, deep-liquidity pools, multi-oracle / Chainlink cross-checks, per-block manipulation caps. TWAP manipulation generally is demonstrated; the specific cadence/sequencer-warping vector is plausible but liquidity-bounded.

seams.trust_assumption: A cross-chain bridge that verifies source-chain events with an on-target light client trusts that a header/proof which passed the source chain's stated finality rule is irreversible, so once the light client accepts a deposit proof at the bridge's configured confirmation depth it irrevocably mints or releases the wrapped asset on the destination chain.
seams.violation: An attacker exploits the gap between the bridge's hard-coded confirmation depth and the source chain's *actual* probabilistic or reconfigurable finality — triggering a deep reorg, a finality-gadget stall, or a validator-set rotation that orphans the proven block after the destination already minted — so a deposit that gets rolled back on the source chain has already been released on the destination, double-spending across the seam that assumes finality is a single global truth.

techniques.name: Source-chain deep reorg after destination mint at fixed confirmation depth
Light-client validator-set rotation orphaning a proven header

seams.rationale: Light-client bridges are sold in 2025-2027 as the trust-minimized successor to multisig bridges, verifying real consensus proofs instead of trusting a federation. The unexamined seam is that 'finality' is not a uniform property: many chains offer only probabilistic finality, some have finality gadgets that can stall or revert under attack, and validator-set rotations can invalidate a header the light client already accepted. Bridges encode a static confirmation depth as a proxy for irreversibility, but that proxy can be wrong exactly when an attacker forces a reorg deeper than the configured threshold, and the destination mint is irreversible while the source deposit is not. This is distinct from the validator-quorum-forgery bridge hint because the cryptographic proof is honest and the quorum is not forged — the violated trust is the equivalence between 'passed my confirmation depth' and 'final on the source chain.' No bridge tooling adapts confirmation depth to live reorg risk, and no detector reconciles a destination mint against a subsequent source-chain reorg that orphaned its proof.
seams.suggested_research: Build a cross-chain finality reconciler that tracks every bridge mint and watches the source chain for reorgs/finality reversions that orphan the proving block, raising a clawback alarm; model the economic conditions under which renting hashpower or stalling a finality gadget makes a reorg-after-mint profitable against named light-client bridges, and prototype dynamic confirmation depth keyed to live reorg depth.

validation.validation_method: On a testnet, deploy a bridge with an on-target light client minting at a fixed confirmation depth N. Deposit on source, let destination mint at depth N, then induce a source-chain reorg deeper than N (or a finality-gadget stall / validator-set rotation orphaning the proven block). Observe whether the destination retains the minted/released asset while the source deposit is rolled back, yielding a double-spend across the seam.
validation.falsifier: The light client requires economic/BFT finality (not just a depth heuristic) and rejects or reverses any proof whose source block does not reach irreversible finality, so a reorg cannot leave a minted asset standing.
validation.prerequisites: Ability to cause/observe a source-chain reorg deeper than the bridge's confirmation depth (PoW hashpower, validator influence, or a chain with reconfigurable/probabilistic finality); a bridge using fixed-depth confirmation. Bridge finality/reorg exploits are a documented incident class.
validation.existing_controls: Waiting for true finality (e.g., Casper FFG finalized checkpoints) rather than fixed depth, conservative confirmation depths, fraud-proof/optimistic windows, pausing mint on reorg detection.

seams.trust_assumption: Users and integrators of an upgradeable proxy contract trust that the address they audited keeps its meaning across upgrades because the proxy's storage layout is append-only and the implementation is reviewed, so they treat the contract's persistent state (balances, roles, allowances) as stable and continue interacting with the same address after an upgrade without re-auditing how the new logic interprets existing storage slots.
seams.violation: A proxy admin (or an attacker who captured the admin/upgrade key) ships a new implementation whose storage layout silently re-maps existing slots — reusing a slot that previously held a balance as a role flag, or shifting a struct so an attacker-controlled value now occupies the owner slot — so the same on-chain bytes that meant one thing under the audited logic are reinterpreted as authority or value under the new logic, with no bytecode-immutability violation visible at the trusted address.

techniques.name: Upgrade-time storage-slot re-interpretation seizing role/owner
Proxy implementation layout shift colliding persistent state

seams.rationale: Upgradeable proxies (transparent, UUPS, beacon, diamond) are ubiquitous in 2025-2027 DeFi and the standard auditing mental model is 'audit the implementation, trust the admin not to rug.' The under-covered seam is not the well-known admin-key-swaps-logic risk but the format/boundary failure at the storage layer: persistent storage outlives the code that defined its layout, and an upgrade can reinterpret the *same bytes* as a different type or field — turning a stale balance slot into a role bitmap or pushing attacker data into the owner slot — without ever appearing as a bytecode change at the address users trust. This is a P5 reinterpretation distinct from the generic proxy-admin-key hint (which is about swapping logic wholesale) because here the attack is the silent semantic re-slotting of existing state that even a fresh audit of the new implementation may miss if it is not diffed against the live storage layout. Layout-diff tooling exists for deploys but is rarely enforced on live state, and no monitor alerts users that an upgrade changed the meaning of slots they hold value in.
seams.suggested_research: Build a live storage-layout differ that, on every observed proxy upgrade, diffs the new implementation's slot map against the prior one over the contract's actual occupied storage and flags any slot whose type/semantics changed under live value; demonstrate a benign-looking upgrade that re-slots a balance mapping into a role flag to seize control without altering the trusted address.

validation.validation_method: Deploy an upgradeable proxy (Transparent/UUPS) with implementation V1 using a given storage layout. Upgrade to V2 whose layout reuses or shifts a slot so that a slot previously holding a balance/struct field is now interpreted as a role flag or owner address. Verify on-chain that the same storage bytes now confer authority/value under V2 with no bytecode change at the trusted address. Real if the reslotting silently transfers role/owner or misvalues balances.
validation.falsifier: The project uses namespaced storage (ERC-7201) or append-only layout with automated layout-diff checks (OpenZeppelin upgrades plugin storage-gap/layout validation) in CI that blocks incompatible upgrades, and upgrade authority is timelocked/multisig-governed with re-audit, so a colliding layout cannot ship unnoticed.
validation.prerequisites: Control of the proxy admin/upgrade key (or its compromise) and an upgrade process lacking storage-layout-compatibility checks; users continuing to trust the address post-upgrade without re-audit.
validation.existing_controls: OpenZeppelin Upgrades storage-layout validation, ERC-7201 namespaced storage, storage gaps, and upgrade timelocks/multisig. Storage-collision bugs are a demonstrated class (audited findings/incidents). Tooling to detect layout incompatibility is available; malicious-intent reslotting via key compromise is the residual risk.

seams.trust_assumption: A contract that accepts an EIP-712 typed signature (permit, meta-transaction, gasless order, intent) trusts that the domain separator binding the signature to one chainId and one verifying contract guarantees the user's authorization can only be exercised in the single context the user signed for, so it executes the signed action as proof of scoped intent.
seams.violation: An attacker replays a validly-signed EIP-712 message in a context the domain separator failed to actually pin to — a fork or rollup that shares the source chain's historical chainId, a redeployed verifying contract at the same address on a second chain via CREATE2, or a wallet that cached a stale domain across a chainId change — so a signature the human scoped to one chain/contract authorizes value movement on another, because the domain binding the verifier relies on collides across the multichain namespace the user never reasoned about.

techniques.name: EIP-712 signature replay across shared/forked chainId domain
CREATE2-redeployed verifier domain collision cross-chain

seams.rationale: EIP-712 typed signing is the backbone of 2025-2027 gasless UX, intents, and account abstraction, and the domain separator (name, version, chainId, verifyingContract) is treated as a complete replay guard. The seam is that the domain's uniqueness assumptions break in the multichain reality the standard predates: forks reuse historical chainIds, deterministic CREATE2 deploys put the same verifying contract address on many chains, and wallets cache domain data across network switches — so a signature scoped to a single context can be exercised in a colliding one. This is broader than the signature-replay hint's single-direction framing because it identifies the structural source of the collision (the multichain namespace versus a separator computed from values that are not globally unique) and includes the wallet-side stale-domain vector. It is a P2 identity/authority seam: the verifier trusts the domain as proof of scoped human intent. There is no tooling that audits a verifying contract's domain for cross-chain/fork collision exposure, and no detection correlates the same signed digest being consumed on two chains.
seams.suggested_research: Build a domain-collision scanner that, for a given verifying contract, enumerates chains/forks where its chainId+address+name+version separator collides and flags exploitable replay surface; demonstrate cross-chain consumption of a single permit signature against a CREATE2-redeployed verifier, and propose a wallet-side domain-freshness check that re-derives chainId at sign time.

validation.validation_method: Deploy a contract accepting EIP-712 signatures (e.g., a permit or meta-tx) and obtain a user signature. Test replay vectors on a testnet: (1) deploy the same contract via CREATE2 at the identical address on a second chain and submit the same signature; (2) cache a domain separator computed once and not recomputed after a chainId change (fork); (3) a chain sharing the source chainId. Observe whether the signature validates and moves value in a context the user did not intend.
validation.falsifier: The contract computes the domain separator dynamically with the live block.chainid (per EIP-712/EIP-1344 guidance) and a unique verifyingContract, includes a nonce, so a signature scoped to chain/contract A reverts on B — no cross-context replay succeeds.
validation.prerequisites: A user signature and a verifying contract that caches/hardcodes chainId in its domain separator or is redeployed at the same address cross-chain (CREATE2). Real, with documented historical incidents of domain-separator/chainId mishandling.
validation.existing_controls: EIP-1344 dynamic chainId in domain separator, recomputation on fork, unique verifyingContract per deployment, nonces, and audits explicitly checking domain-separator caching. This is a well-documented smart-contract pitfall (e.g., known DAI/permit domain caching discussions, fork-replay analyses); detection via static analysis (Slither-style) is partial.

seams.trust_assumption: A wallet user relies on the transaction-simulation / clear-signing preview the wallet renders (asset changes, recipients, decoded calldata) as a faithful prediction of what executing the transaction will do, so they approve based on the human-readable summary rather than the opaque calldata, treating 'simulation shows I receive X and send Y' as binding ground truth for on-chain effect.
seams.violation: An attacker crafts calldata whose simulated execution diverges from its real execution — exploiting state that changes between simulation and inclusion (oracle/price reads, block.timestamp/number, a front-run state mutation, or a contract that branches on tx.origin/gasleft or a flash-set storage flag the simulator doesn't reproduce) — so the wallet's preview shows a benign, profitable-looking outcome while the mined transaction drains the signer, weaponizing the very simulation layer that was built to end blind signing.

techniques.name: Simulation/execution state divergence yielding decoy preview
Calldata branching on context absent in wallet simulation

seams.rationale: Clear-signing and transaction simulation are the 2025-2027 industry answer to the blind-signing problem, and wallets/security extensions now present a decoded preview that users are explicitly told to trust over raw calldata. The under-explored seam is that a simulation is an evaluation against a snapshot of state, and on-chain execution happens later against different state under attacker influence — so a contract can be written to behave one way under the wallet's simulation conditions and another way at inclusion (reading a price the attacker will move, branching on a storage flag set by a sandwiching tx, or on tx ordering). The violated trust is data->control: the human treats the preview data as controlling their decision, while the attacker controls the gap between previewed and realized state. This is a distinct, newly-relevant seam created by the clear-signing rollout itself (the blind-signing hint assumed no preview at all); no wallet flags simulation/execution divergence risk, and no detection compares a signed tx's simulated preview to its mined effect.
seams.suggested_research: Build a wallet-side divergence guard that re-simulates at inclusion-adjacent state (or against adversarial state perturbations) and warns when the realized outcome can deviate from the shown preview; demonstrate a contract whose simulation shows a gain but whose mined execution drains the signer by reading attacker-moved state, and quantify how many production wallets surface no divergence warning.

validation.validation_method: Deploy a malicious contract whose execution branches on state that differs between wallet simulation and on-chain inclusion (e.g., block.timestamp/number, an oracle price read, tx.origin, or a storage flag flipped by a front-run). Build a transaction whose simulated preview shows benign/profitable asset changes, sign it through a simulating wallet, and observe the mined transaction draining the signer despite the benign preview.
validation.falsifier: The wallet's simulation reproduces the real execution state (pins block/oracle conditions or simulates at inclusion) such that the preview matches the on-chain effect, or the divergence cannot be made reliable enough to flip outcome between preview and inclusion.
validation.prerequisites: Ability to deploy a contract and craft calldata; a target wallet that renders simulation previews; control of conditions enabling state divergence (e.g., front-run capability or time/oracle dependence).
validation.existing_controls: Simulation-divergence and decoy-preview drainer techniques are documented by wallet security teams (Blockaid, Wallet Guard, MetaMask security blogs); mitigations include warning on divergence-prone patterns, multiple-block simulation, and approval-scope warnings. Drainer ecosystems actively exploit related tricks.

seams.trust_assumption: A verifier of a W3C verifiable credential trusts that checking the issuer's signature plus the referenced status mechanism (StatusList2021 bitstring, on-chain revocation registry, did:method resolution) tells it the credential is currently valid, so a credential that resolves and whose status bit reads 'active' is accepted as a present-tense attestation of the holder's standing.
seams.violation: An attacker exploits the propagation and resolution latency in the decentralized status layer — presenting a credential after revocation but before the status list/registry the verifier caches has updated, or steering the verifier to a stale did:method resolution or an outdated status-list mirror — so a revoked or rotated credential is honored as active, because in a decentralized identity system there is no synchronous authority that can answer 'is this valid right now' and the verifier silently trusts a snapshot that lags the true state.

techniques.name: Post-revocation credential acceptance during status-list propagation lag
Stale did/status-list resolution steered to honor revoked VC

seams.rationale: Decentralized identity (DIDs and W3C verifiable credentials) is moving from pilots to real deployment in 2025-2027 (EU digital identity wallets, reusable KYC, employment/age credentials), and the verification model deliberately removes the central authority that PKI/OCSP-style systems queried for live status. The seam is that revocation in this world is eventually-consistent: status lists are published artifacts that verifiers fetch and cache, did:method resolution can be cached or steered, and there is no synchronous 'check now' — so the window between revocation and propagation is a designed-in feature, not a bug, that an attacker times against. The violated trust is the provenance/freshness of the status answer the verifier relies on. This is distinct from classic cert-revocation-latency because the status authority is itself decentralized and the verifier often cannot even reach a canonical current source. No DID wallet/verifier tooling measures or bounds status freshness, and no detection flags credentials presented during their revocation-propagation window.
seams.suggested_research: Measure real-world status-propagation latency across StatusList2021 and on-chain revocation registries in shipping VC stacks, and build a verifier-side freshness bound that rejects or down-trusts credentials whose status snapshot is older than a policy threshold; demonstrate acceptance of a just-revoked credential by presenting it inside the propagation window against a caching verifier.

validation.validation_method: Set up a VC verifier using StatusList2021 (or an on-chain revocation registry) that caches the status list. Issue a credential, revoke it at the issuer, then present the credential to the verifier within the cache TTL / before the status list mirror updates. Observe whether the verifier accepts the revoked credential as active. Vary cache TTL and force resolution to a stale mirror to measure the acceptance window.
validation.falsifier: The verifier fetches a fresh, signed, timestamped status list on every verification (no stale cache) or uses a freshness/'valid as of' bound, so a credential revoked before presentation is reliably rejected; revocation propagation is effectively synchronous in the deployment.
validation.prerequisites: Ability to present a credential during the propagation/cache window, or to steer the verifier to a stale status-list mirror / did resolution. Window may be short.
validation.existing_controls: Short status-list cache TTLs, signed and freshness-bounded status lists, OCSP-stapling-style freshness, online status checks. Revocation-propagation lag is a known general PKI/CRL problem; specific VC StatusList exploitation is plausible, not widely demonstrated.

seams.trust_assumption: A DAO's on-chain governance trusts that a token (or vote-escrowed/delegated) balance measured at a proposal's snapshot block represents a participant's genuine, at-risk economic stake, so it equates voting weight at that block with legitimate skin-in-the-game and lets that measured weight decide treasury, parameter, and upgrade outcomes.
seams.violation: An attacker assembles voting weight they have no lasting stake in — flash-borrowing governance tokens, renting delegated voting power, or momentarily acquiring vote-escrow positions just before the snapshot block and unwinding immediately after — so the snapshot reads attacker-controlled weight as legitimate stake and passes a self-dealing proposal, because the governance system trusts a point-in-time balance as a proxy for durable economic alignment it never verifies the persistence of.

techniques.name: Flash-borrowed governance-token snapshot vote capture
Rented delegated voting power transient to the snapshot block

seams.rationale: On-chain DAO governance controls enormous treasuries and protocol-upgrade keys in 2025-2027, and the security model assumes voting weight equals economic stake equals aligned incentives. The seam is that balance at a snapshot block is a fungible, rentable, flash-loanable quantity — markets now exist to borrow governance tokens and rent delegated/vote-escrow power for a single block — so an attacker can present overwhelming 'stake' at the measurement instant with zero durable exposure, then unwind. The violated trust is the identity/authority equivalence between a transient measured balance and persistent skin-in-the-game. While the governance-flash-borrow hint names the primitive, the under-tooled extension here is that even time-locked or vote-escrow and delegation designs are gameable via rental markets and just-in-time escrow, and there is no tooling that scores a proposal's passing coalition for stake transience. No governance framework verifies durable holding across the proposal lifecycle, and no monitor flags votes whose weight was borrowed/rented around the snapshot.
seams.suggested_research: Build a governance-stake-persistence analyzer that, for each passed proposal, traces whether the deciding voters held their weight durably or acquired/borrowed it around the snapshot and unwound after, scoring coalition transience; prototype a time-integrated voting weight (stake held across a window, not a single block) and demonstrate capture of a vanilla snapshot vote via flash-rented delegation.

validation.validation_method: On a testnet DAO whose voting weight is read from token balance at a snapshot block within the same flash-loan-able transaction (no time-lock/escrow lock), flash-borrow governance tokens, cast a self-dealing vote at the snapshot, and unwind in the same transaction. Observe whether the snapshot counted the borrowed weight and the proposal passed.
validation.falsifier: Governance measures weight via a past/lagged snapshot block (checkpointed before the proposal, e.g., ERC20Votes getPastVotes) or requires locked vote-escrow, so flash-borrowed weight present only momentarily is not counted.
validation.prerequisites: A DAO that snapshots weight at a manipulable/current block and a deep flash-loan market for the governance token. Flash-loan governance attacks (e.g., Beanstalk) are a documented real-world incident class.
validation.existing_controls: Past-block vote snapshots (ERC20Votes), vote-escrow with lockups, proposal time-locks, quorum + delay, flash-loan-resistant snapshot timing.

seams.trust_assumption: An ERC-4337 paymaster that agrees to sponsor a user operation's gas trusts that the conditions it checked in the validation phase — the userOp's calldata, the account's state, an off-chain signed sponsorship policy, or a token allowance it will pull as repayment — still hold when the operation executes, so it commits to paying gas based on the picture it validated.
seams.violation: An attacker exploits the structural separation between ERC-4337's validation and execution phases (and the bundler's freedom to order/aggregate userOps) so that state the paymaster relied on during validation is changed before or during execution — the repayment token is drained, the sponsored action is swapped via reentrant or ordering effects, or aggregated userOps interfere — leaving the paymaster to pay gas for an operation that no longer matches what it agreed to sponsor, draining its deposit at scale.

techniques.name: Validation/execution state divergence draining paymaster deposit
Bundler ordering/aggregation invalidating sponsorship precondition

seams.rationale: Account abstraction via ERC-4337 is hitting production wallets and chains in 2025-2027, and paymasters that sponsor gas are central to the gasless UX everyone is building.
