register.falsifier: The malicious reuse still alerts at normal severity because retraining is gated by labeled-incident review, drift checks, or the suppression keys on features the attacker cannot fully control (so FP closure does not transfer to the malicious instance).

register.rationale: Agents increasingly install skills/plugins by name from registries with little vetting. Dependency confusion and typosquatting transfer directly, but the blast radius is worse: the installed skill runs with composed agent+host authority and can write the agent's memory for persistence. This is the explicit §5.2 'supply-chain x agent-skill typosquat-at-install' diagonal, decomposed from the capability-composition seam.
register.suggested_research: Crawl emerging agent-skill registries for namespace-squatting exposure; test resolver precedence (public vs private); propose signed, namespaced, install-time-reviewed skill manifests.
register.validation_method: On a registry/marketplace that an agent installs skills/plugins from by name (npm-backed MCP servers, plugin marketplaces, pip-installed tools), publish a package whose name is one edit-distance from a popular skill (e.g. 'githb-mcp' vs 'github-mcp'). Have an agent resolve and install the named skill via a fuzzy or unscoped name. Confirm the typosquat installs and that its install/postinstall or first tool call runs with the agent's host privileges. Real if the wrong-author package is fetched and gains execution.
register.falsifier: The registry enforces author/namespace scoping, signed provenance, or the install path requires an exact pinned identifier (scope+name+version+integrity hash), so a near-name does not resolve to the attacker package and/or install scripts do not run with agent authority.

register.rationale: Unlike MFA push fatigue, the conditioning here is endogenous: the legitimate agent workflow generates so many trivially-safe approval prompts that the human's vigilance decays as a direct function of the product's own design, making the eventual malicious approval a feature of normal operation rather than an external bombing campaign. Vendors treat HITL as a sufficient safety control and instrument approval rate as a success metric, not as an attack surface; there is no tooling that models approval-prompt salience or measures operator habituation, and no detection that distinguishes a deliberated approval from a conditioned reflex. The seam is the assumption that a click equals judgment.
register.suggested_research: Run a study measuring approval latency and error rate as a function of prior benign-prompt volume; design and test 'salience budgeting' (rate-limiting/escalating friction for consequential actions) and detect anomalous fast-approval patterns as a habituation signal.
register.validation_method: Run a controlled human-subjects-style trial: present operators a stream of agent confirmation prompts where the overwhelming majority are benign/identical, then inject a single materially different malicious action mid-stream. Measure approval rate and dwell time on the malicious prompt vs a control group seeing it first. A statistically higher approval rate / lower scrutiny under the high-frequency benign stream confirms conditioning.
register.falsifier: Operators detect and reject the injected malicious action at the same rate regardless of preceding benign-confirmation volume, i.e., approval fatigue does not measurably degrade scrutiny in the workflow.

register.rationale: The MCP authorization spec leans on OAuth 2.1 dynamic client registration so agents can connect to arbitrary servers without pre-provisioning, which removes the human vetting step that traditional OAuth app onboarding relied on; the authorization server now grants tokens to clients with no established identity or reputation. The risk is amplified when the consenting party is an agent or a habituated user clicking through an agent-initiated flow. Enterprise OAuth governance tooling (app consent dashboards, publisher verification) assumes a finite set of pre-registered apps and does not model a stream of ephemeral self-registered MCP clients; there is no detection for anomalous DCR volume or for tokens held by clients that lack a verifiable publisher.
register.suggested_research: Inventory how major IdPs/AS implementations handle MCP DCR (rate limits, publisher attestation, consent UX); demonstrate a rogue-MCP token-grab and prototype DCR reputation/attestation gating plus anomalous-registration detection.
register.validation_method: Against an OAuth 2.1 AS that supports RFC 7591 dynamic client registration for MCP, self-register a client with a benign display name/logo and a redirect URI you control. Drive a user (or an over-eager agent) through the consent flow and confirm you receive scoped, refreshable tokens with no out-of-band vetting of the client. Real if an unvetted ephemeral client obtains durable delegated authority purely via self-registration plus habituated consent.
register.falsifier: The AS gates DCR behind admin approval, software statements / attestation (RFC 7591 signed software statements), publisher verification, or restricts registration to allowlisted issuers, so an arbitrary self-registered client cannot reach a consent screen with sensitive scopes.

register.rationale: The 2025-2027 safety-eval ecosystem rewards visible benchmark scores, so models and guardrails are tuned until they pass widely-published evals; because those evals are finite and public, defenders unwittingly train to the test, and the certified-safe artifact is brittle precisely on the inputs the benchmark did not enumerate. This is a provenance seam: the eval result is trusted as evidence about a behavior class it cannot actually guarantee. Standard practice has no tooling to measure the gap between eval coverage and intended behavior, and no detection fires when an input lands in the uncovered region, because by construction it looks like exactly the safe behavior the model demonstrated on the benchmark.
register.suggested_research: Build a coverage-vs-intent differential: hold out adversarial paraphrases semantically equivalent to eval items but outside their surface distribution, and measure refusal-rate collapse across released models/guardrails to quantify per-eval overfit.
register.validation_method: Take a model/guardrail with a published safety benchmark. Reproduce the benchmark to confirm it passes. Then construct held-out inputs that target the same harmful intent but are stylistically/structurally outside the benchmark's distribution (paraphrase, obfuscation, novel framing) and measure refusal rate on these vs. the published set. The seam is shown if refusal collapses materially on the out-of-distribution-but-same-intent set while the published eval still scores high (Goodhart gap).
register.falsifier: Refusal rates on out-of-distribution same-intent inputs match the published eval (the guardrail generalizes to intent, not surface form), so no exploitable distribution gap exists.

register.rationale: Adapters are tiny, abundant, and shared on open hubs with far weaker provenance hygiene than base models, yet they can fully rewrite a model's safety behavior at low cost; deployers reason about the base model's reputation and forget that the runtime artifact is base+adapter. Unlike a poisoned dependency in code, there is no source to diff, signatures rarely cover adapter weights, and the malicious behavior can be made conditional so it never appears in pre-deployment testing. Current model-supply-chain tooling focuses on full-checkpoint hashing and license scanning; there is no practical adapter-level integrity attestation or behavioral-delta auditing, and no detection that the loaded composition differs in safety from the base lineage it claims.
register.suggested_research: Develop adapter behavioral-delta fingerprinting (compare base vs base+adapter refusal/behavior on a probe battery) and a signed adapter-provenance scheme; survey adapters on public hubs for trigger-conditioned alignment regressions.
register.validation_method: Fine-tune a LoRA adapter on a vetted base model with a trigger-conditioned backdoor (benign on normal inputs, alignment-stripped or attacker-behavior on a chosen trigger string). Distribute/load it as a 'behavioral delta' onto the base, then evaluate: (1) normal eval suite passes and looks like the trusted base; (2) on the trigger, safety/refusal behavior is overridden. Confirms composition silently inherits base trust while activating hidden behavior.
register.falsifier: The adapter cannot encode a reliable trigger-gated safety override that survives the base model's safety layer, OR loading pipeline runs an adapter-level safety/behavioral diff eval that flags the deviation before deployment.

register.rationale: Computer-use agents (Claude/OpenAI operator-style, browser-use frameworks) ground actions in a screenshot plus an accessibility/DOM snapshot, but the agent's perception of the page and a human's perception are produced by entirely different pipelines, so a page can be crafted to read one way to a vision model and another to a person. Existing web-security tooling checks server-side content and existing prompt-injection filters scan text strings, but neither models the gap between rendered pixels, the accessibility tree the agent ingests, and the action the agent ultimately commits; clickjacking defenses (framebusting, X-Frame-Options) assume a human victim's eyes, not a model's attention map. There is essentially no tooling that diffs 'what the agent perceived' against 'what executing the click actually does,' and no detection that flags a perception/action mismatch.
register.suggested_research: Build a harness that renders adversarial pages, captures the agent's screenshot+accessibility-tree, records the chosen action, and measures divergence between human-visible affordance and committed action; quantify how many leading computer-use agents can be steered to click a destructive control hidden from the human render.
register.validation_method: Build a page where a 'Confirm/Cancel' control's accessibility-tree label or off-viewport DOM text differs from human-visible rendering (CSS-hidden but a11y-exposed text, an overlay, or an adversarial patch on the screenshot region). Run a computer-use/browser-use agent (e.g., a vision+DOM agent) against it on a benign task and observe whether it clicks/acts based on the injected label while a human watching the rendered pixels would understand a different action.
register.falsifier: The agent consistently acts on the same rendered state a human sees (e.g., it grounds on screenshot pixels with viewport clipping and ignores hidden a11y text), so the injected divergence does not change its action.

register.rationale: To cut token and API costs, agent frameworks (and prompt-caching layers) increasingly memoize tool outputs keyed on call arguments, but this turns a one-time output-influence into persistent control: the upstream source can be fixed and the agent still consumes the poisoned snapshot. This differs from vector-store or memory persistence because the carrier is the ephemeral-by-assumption tool-result cache, which has no provenance or freshness semantics and is rarely scoped per-tenant. No current tooling treats tool-result caches as a trust surface, there is no integrity/TTL discipline tied to source freshness, and SOC telemetry does not record cache hits as a distinct event, so a poisoned result is invisible after the originating fetch scrolls out of logs.
register.suggested_research: Audit popular agent frameworks for tool-result cache scope, key derivation, and TTL; demonstrate a poison-once-serve-many attack and prototype a provenance-stamped cache that re-validates against source freshness before a hit is trusted.
register.validation_method: Configure an agent runtime that caches tool/function-call results keyed on tool name + arguments (common cost optimization). Point a tool at an attacker-controllable source (a web URL or a mock MCP server). Action: have the tool fetch the resource once while it serves a poisoned response, populating the cache. Then clean the upstream source. Observe in subsequent runs/sessions/users whether the cached poisoned result is re-served as authoritative instead of re-fetching the now-clean source.
register.falsifier: The runtime re-executes the tool (cache miss, short TTL, or no result caching) and returns the clean upstream response; or cache keys are scoped per-session/per-user so poison cannot cross to other users; or tool results are not cached at all.

register.rationale: Detection-as-code moved the SOC from hand-tuned rules to git-synced community rule repos (Sigma HQ, Elastic detection-rules, Sentinel content hub) reviewed by humans who check syntax and intent prose, not adversarial coverage. A reviewer cannot easily tell that an added `filter` or a renamed field reduces true-positive coverage to zero while the rule still parses, deploys, and shows green in MITRE-coverage heatmaps. This is a provenance seam: the SOC trusts the rule for where it came from, not for what it actually matches against real events. No tooling differentially tests a rule's firing surface before/after a PR, and the blind spot persists unnoticed until the excluded TTP is used.
register.suggested_research: Build a regression harness that replays a labeled event corpus against rule PRs and flags coverage deltas; mine Sigma/Elastic repo history for exclusion clauses correlated with known TTPs and measure mean time-to-detect a deliberately weakening PR.
register.validation_method: Submit a PR to a detection-as-code repo (or a fork in a lab) adding a Sigma rule whose title/metadata claim broad TTP coverage but whose filter contains an over-scoped exclusion or a wrong field mapping that silently drops the target behavior. Run the rule against a dataset containing the malicious behavior and confirm zero alerts while coverage dashboards/CI lint show the rule present and green.
register.falsifier: CI/test harness (rule unit tests with positive/negative event fixtures, e.g., Sigma's test framework or detection validation against attack telemetry) catches the coverage gap and fails the merge, so the poisoned rule cannot ship looking green.

register.rationale: Zero-trust adoption made device posture a primary access gate, but the posture is frequently client-asserted (an agent reports 'compliant') rather than hardware-rooted, and even where a TPM is involved the binding between the attested boot state and the live runtime posture is weak. The IdP authorizes the asserted position, not verified ground truth. Defenders display posture as authoritative in dashboards and gate sensitive apps on it, yet there is little public tooling that systematically forges or replays each vendor's compliance signal, and almost no detection that cross-checks asserted posture against independent server-side observation. Defense automated the gate faster than it rooted the signal.
register.suggested_research: Inventory which posture attributes per major MDM/IdP (Intune, Jamf, Okta Device Trust) are client-asserted vs hardware-attested; build a posture-spoofing test agent and propose server-corroborated posture as a control.
register.validation_method: On a device enrolled in a conditional-access stack where posture is reported by a software agent (not hardware-attested), tamper with the agent's reported state (patch the binary, hook its API, edit local compliance cache) or replay a previously captured known-good signed posture payload, then attempt access. Real if the IdP applies the trusted-device policy branch and waives step-up despite the endpoint being non-compliant/compromised.
register.falsifier: Posture is bound to hardware attestation (TPM/Secure Enclave, device-bound keys, Windows attested compliance, Android Play Integrity / Apple App Attest) so tampered or replayed client claims fail cryptographic verification at the IdP.

register.rationale: Organizations replaced standing admin with JIT/PAM elevation (Entra PIM, AWS IAM Identity Center, CyberArk, Teleport) on the premise that approval workflow plus time-boxing equals control. But the workflow trusts the approval event as proof of intent, and the seams are everywhere: auto-approval rules, self-approval in thin teams, approvers rubber-stamping AI-plausible justifications, and sessions/tokens that outlive the JIT window. The system authorizes the approved position, not the actual use, and records the abuse as a legitimate grant, which corrupts the audit trail defenders rely on. There is little tooling that probes a tenant's JIT auto-approval and window-race surface, and detection of post-expiry residual-session abuse is nascent.
register.suggested_research: Map JIT/PAM products' auto-approval, self-approval, and token-vs-grant lifetime mismatches; build a tester for activation-window races and residual-session persistence after JIT deactivation, and quantify approver susceptibility to LLM-generated justifications.
register.validation_method: In a JIT PAM system (e.g., a privileged-access workflow with justification + approver), test each abuse path: (1) configure or discover an auto-approval rule and request elevation that matches it; (2) request elevation as a member of a group that can approve its own requests (self-approval); (3) race the activation/deactivation window to retain access after nominal expiry; (4) submit a plausible justification to a human approver (social-engineering, out of scope for pure technical PoC but observable in tabletop). Observe whether elevated privilege is granted and recorded as legitimate, and whether the session is usable outside the justified scope.
register.falsifier: The PAM enforces separation of duties (no self-approval), no auto-approval for privileged scopes, hard session revocation at expiry with no race window, and scope-binding of the grant to the justification — so a procedurally approved elevation cannot be turned into unscoped or standing privilege.

register.rationale: Service meshes (Istio, Linkerd, Consul) made mTLS-everywhere cheap, and teams adopted it as the security story, but mTLS proves who the peer is, not what the peer may do at L7. Many meshes default to peer-authn with permissive or absent fine-grained authorization policy, and forwarded-identity headers are trusted inside the trust boundary. A single compromised workload inherits the mesh-wide ability to reach services its application-layer entitlements never granted. This is a context-inheritance seam: downstream inherits the transport's authenticated trust without re-validating L7 intent. Tooling to map mesh authz gaps and forge forwarded-identity is nascent, and detection of intra-mesh L7 authz violations is rare because the traffic is encrypted-and-trusted.
register.suggested_research: Build a mesh-authz mapper that diffs configured AuthorizationPolicies against the actual service call graph to find peer-authenticated-but-unauthorized edges; test forwarded-identity header trust between sidecars.
register.validation_method: In an Istio/Linkerd mesh, configure a downstream service that authorizes solely on the authenticated mTLS peer identity (PeerAuthentication / namespace) but lacks AuthorizationPolicy rules on path/method. From a low-privilege but valid in-mesh workload, send requests to privileged paths/methods and inject forwarded-identity headers (X-Forwarded-Client-Cert spoof, propagated JWT replay). Real if the downstream grants access the L7 policy should deny.
register.falsifier: AuthorizationPolicies enforce path/method/JWT-claim rules per caller, sidecars strip/overwrite client-supplied forwarded-identity headers, and propagated tokens are audience/nonce-bound, so peer identity does not by itself authorize L7 actions.

register.rationale: The ecosystem rushed to 'verify provenance' (Sigstore, SLSA, in-toto, GitHub artifact attestations) and admission controllers now gate on a green signature check. But verification proves the attestation is authentic and well-formed, not that its scope covers the step the attacker abused: dependency resolution, base-image selection, a hermeticity gap, or a digest that binds the wrong subject. Defenders read 'provenance verified' as 'artifact trustworthy', a provenance seam where the receiver checks the label, not the thing. Public tooling to audit the coverage gap between an attestation's predicate and the real build DAG is nascent, and nothing detects a deploy that passed verification while the exploited step lay outside the claim.
register.suggested_research: Survey real SLSA/in-toto predicates in the wild for which build steps they actually bind; build a scope-coverage linter that diffs the attested DAG against the full build and flags unattested influence on the final digest.
register.validation_method: Produce a build with a valid SLSA/in-toto provenance attestation, then introduce malice outside the predicate's binding scope: a vendored dependency not enumerated in the materials, a post-build artifact mutation that changes bytes but where the verifier only checks a loosely-scoped subject digest, or a pre-build code-gen step the predicate omits. Action: submit the artifact to an admission controller / deploy gate / package consumer that verifies the attestation. Observe whether verification passes despite the malicious portion being outside the attested claim.
register.falsifier: The verifier binds the exact deployed artifact digest as the attestation subject and the predicate fully enumerates materials/build steps such that the malicious portion would change the verified digest or fail policy — so the tampered artifact fails verification.

register.rationale: SOC teams adopted no-touch SOAR/auto-remediation faster than they hardened the data path into it: the playbook author assumes alert fields are trustworthy because they came from the EDR/SIEM, but those fields are populated from attacker-influenced artifacts (process names, file paths, web logs). The defender's own privileged automation runner now executes against parameters the adversary chose, and because the action is a 'response' it is allowlisted, unlogged as suspicious, and runs with broad cross-platform tokens. There is no public tooling that fuzzes the alert-field-to-action expansion and almost no detection that treats a remediation action as a potential injection sink.
register.suggested_research: Build a harness that seeds malicious values into observable artifact types (filename, username, UA, DNS) and traces which fields reach SOAR action templates unescaped in Splunk SOAR/Sentinel/Tines/Cortex XSOAR; propose taint-tracking from telemetry ingestion to playbook action sinks.
register.validation_method: Build a SOAR playbook (e.g., in a test instance) that auto-remediates by interpolating alert fields (hostname, username, filename, URL, process command line) into API calls or shell/OS commands without parameterization. Action: generate telemetry containing an attacker-controlled field carrying a payload (e.g., a username like `; curl evil` or an OS command-injection / API-parameter-injection string). Observe whether the playbook expands the payload into the remediation action (command executed, unintended API call fired, wrong asset isolated).
register.falsifier: The playbook parameterizes inputs (no string interpolation into commands), validates/escapes alert fields, or the action APIs reject the malformed identifiers — so the injected payload is treated as inert data, not executed.

register.rationale: Cert lifecycle automation is increasingly delegated to AI ops agents (cloud private-CA, internal ACME, service-mesh identity) in 2025-2026, and the issuance trust model assumes the requester validated domain/identity control. An agent does not perform that validation with adversarial rigor and can be steered by untrusted input into requesting or rubber-stamping certificates for arbitrary names, turning it into a CA confused deputy whose mis-issuance poisons everything that trusts the resulting certs. This is the agent-authority primitive landing on PKI/federation identity, a substrate that very recently became agent-reachable; CT logs offer only nascent after-the-fact detection and there is no preventive tooling that constrains agent-scoped issuance to validated names.
register.suggested_research: Build an issuance broker that hard-binds an agent's cert requests to an out-of-band proof of name control and forbids agent-driven trust-anchor changes without human dual-control; demonstrate ticket injection driving a PKI agent to mis-issue a cert for a name it does not control.
register.validation_method: Give an LLM PKI-ops agent issuance/approval authority against a test internal CA or ACME endpoint. Feed it a ticket/domain string/service descriptor containing injected instructions to request or approve a certificate for a name the requester does not control. Observe whether the agent issues/approves the cert (or installs a trust anchor) for the attacker-chosen name without independent domain-control validation.
register.falsifier: Issuance is still gated by independent domain-control validation (ACME challenge, CAA, RA approval) that the agent cannot satisfy for names it doesn't control, so the agent's confused request fails validation regardless of its intent.

register.rationale: Fleet operators are deploying AI dispatch/ops agents on top of telematics APIs (Geotab, Samsara-class) that expose remote-command primitives, and those APIs were built to trust a human dispatcher behind an OAuth scope. The agent inherits that scope but its 'intent' is derived from untrusted text it ingests (tickets, emails, route data), so a data-to-control injection becomes physical actuation against many vehicles simultaneously, blowing past the one-vehicle-at-a-time human pace the command APIs assumed. No telematics platform today detects agent-originated command bursts or anomalous fan-out, and no automotive IDS sees the cloud-side seam, leaving this AI-to-CAN diagonal entirely untooled.
register.suggested_research: Prototype a telematics-side anomaly gate that profiles human dispatcher command cadence and blocks agent-driven fan-out (e.g., >N immobilizer/unlock commands per minute) pending human re-auth; demonstrate ticket-borne prompt injection driving a fleet agent to mass remote-unlock in a sandbox.
register.validation_method: In a test/staging fleet-telematics environment, give an LLM agent the dispatcher's remote-command scope and feed it a poisoned support ticket/route note containing an injection instructing bulk remote actuation (unlock/remote-start). Observe whether the agent emits the bulk commands and whether the backend relays them as authentic dispatcher intent to (simulated) vehicle gateways.
register.falsifier: The agent's tool to issue remote commands is gated by human approval / rate limits / out-of-band confirmation, OR the backend re-authenticates per-command against a verified dispatcher action, so injected text cannot translate into relayed bulk actuation.

register.rationale: RF presence spoofing (badge cloning, BLE relay) is a mature wireless attack, but the new bridge is that 2025-2026 building/automation agents treat presence telemetry as an authenticated identity-and-context input that drives downstream tool actions, fusing a P3 provenance failure with P2 authority. Classic physical access control at least mapped a badge to one door; an agent generalizes a spoofed presence fact into arbitrary co-located reasoning and actuation. No agent framework today carries provenance/assurance metadata on presence signals or refuses to act on unauthenticated RF as ground truth, so this wireless-to-agent identity diagonal is untooled and undetected.
register.suggested_research: Define a presence-assurance metadata schema (signal type, anti-relay/liveness evidence, confidence) that agents must check before treating RF telemetry as identity, and prototype agent guardrails that downgrade unauthenticated presence to a non-authoritative hint; demonstrate badge-clone-driven agent door-unlock.
register.validation_method: Build an agent that consumes RFID/BLE/Wi-Fi presence telemetry and makes an access/approval decision on 'person X present'. Clone a static 125kHz/13.56MHz badge UID (Proxmark) or relay a BLE token, replay it to the reader feeding the agent, and confirm the agent treats the forged signal as authenticated presence and acts (unlock/approve).
register.falsifier: The agent's presence input uses cryptographically authenticated credentials (e.g., challenge-response smartcards, BLE with mutual auth) or correlates multiple independent signals, so a cloned UID / relayed token does not yield an accepted presence fact.

register.rationale: As agents perform consequential writes across cloud, identity, and SaaS in 2025-2027, organizations rely on audit logs for accountability, but those logs record an unsigned actor assertion rather than a verifiable chain linking the human who approved a goal, the agent that executed it, and the precise action emitted. There is no provenance primitive that makes an agent action non-repudiable, so a compromised or injected agent's actions are forensically indistinguishable from authorized ones, blame lands on the wrong principal, and incident response cannot reconstruct true causation. This provenance seam is distinct from sub-agent context inheritance (which concerns authority flow) because it concerns after-the-fact attribution integrity; no agent framework signs an authorizer->agent->action triple and no detection flags missing or forged provenance.
register.suggested_research: Define a signed action-provenance token that cryptographically binds the human authorization, the agent identity, the tool/parameters, and a nonce, emitted with every consequential action and verifiable by the audit layer; prototype a broker that rejects unprovenanced agent writes and demonstrate forensic attribution under a simulated agent compromise.
register.validation_method: Instrument an agent that performs tool calls / API writes and inspect the resulting audit log entries. Attempt to (a) perform an action via a prompt-injected path and (b) forge/replay a log entry's actor field. Confirm there is no cryptographic binding among the human authorizer, agent identity, and the specific action — i.e., the log is an unsigned assertion attributable to an innocent principal.
register.falsifier: The action pipeline produces a signed, tamper-evident provenance binding (e.g., signed action tokens, workload identity + request signing, transparency log) that ties authorizer-agent-action together and would distinguish a forged/replayed action.

register.rationale: Million-token context windows and persistent agent memory in 2025-2027 mean users and tools routinely place secrets and PII into conversational state that the platform treats as transient, but that state is in fact persisted into transcripts, observability logs, feedback datasets, and exportable history that live far beyond the task and are reachable by integrations, support staff, or attackers. The seam is a time/state mismatch: the data is assumed ephemeral but is durably retained, and unlike the known prompt-cache timing leak this concerns long-lived storage of the cleartext itself, not a side channel. No platform runs secret-scanning/redaction over context and memory the way it would over a code repo, secrets in transcripts are never rotated, and detection of credential persistence in conversational stores is only nascent.
register.suggested_research: Build a context/memory secret-scanner that detects and redacts credentials and PII before persistence, transcript export, or feedback capture, and triggers rotation on detected secrets; quantify secret-leak rates into transcript and observability stores across major assistant platforms.
register.validation_method: Paste a unique canary secret (e.g., a tagged fake API key) into a long-context conversation or agent memory on an assistant platform. Action: trace where it lands — transcript exports, vendor request logs, feedback/training stores, synced chat history, integration-readable memory. Then, from a secondary store (export feature, log access, over-broad integration), attempt to read the canary back after the task ends. Observe whether the secret is recoverable from a secondary store and whether secret-scanning/redaction ever fired.
register.falsifier: The platform secret-scans and redacts conversational/memory state before persistence/export, scopes memory so secondary stores cannot be read by other principals, and applies retention/rotation — so the canary is not recoverable from any secondary store after the task.

register.rationale: Identity and fraud pipelines in 2025-2027 increasingly outsource ground truth to data-broker and enrichment APIs and let agents auto-consume those verdicts, but the enrichment supply chain is loosely governed and partly attacker-influenceable (seeded broker entries, manipulable reputation feeds, declared device attributes), so a manipulated upstream fact silently steers a downstream authority decision. The seam is the pipeline's treatment of enrichment output as authoritative rather than as untrusted attacker-reachable data; this is a data-to-control violation on the identity plane distinct from synthetic-identity aging because here the attacker targets the institution's external truth source rather than building a persona. No pipeline carries provenance/confidence on enrichment facts, cross-validates conflicting providers, or detects feed poisoning, leaving this seam untooled and undetected.
register.suggested_research: Build an enrichment-provenance layer that attaches source, freshness, and corroboration confidence to every third-party fact and requires multi-provider agreement before it can move an identity decision past a risk threshold; demonstrate broker-record seeding that flips an automated KYC/fraud verdict and measure detectability.
register.validation_method: In an identity/risk pipeline that ingests a data-broker or reputation/IP-intel feed, seed attacker-favorable records upstream (create false firmographic/broker entries, age an IP/domain to gain reputation, register misleading device attributes) and submit a subject that triggers the enrichment lookup. Observe whether the poisoned 'facts' flip an automated approve/deny/escalate decision. Seam confirmed if the manipulated feed changes the decision and the pipeline cannot trace the poisoned source.
register.falsifier: The pipeline corroborates enrichment against independent signals or treats single-source enrichment as advisory only (human review, multi-source consensus), so a single poisoned feed cannot move the decision.

register.rationale: Sovereignty programs in 2025-2027 pin storage and indexes to jurisdictions, but the generated output of a model is a derived artifact that carries no residency provenance, and multi-region agent routing or a cross-region fallback endpoint will happily synthesize regulated source data into a response that lands outside the boundary. Unlike classic data-at-rest controls, there is no mechanism that propagates a residency label from a vector chunk or memory record into the tokens that quote it, no detection that a cross-region inference call returned in-region regulated content, and no tooling to block residency-violating synthesis. This format-boundary seam between governed sources and ungoverned generated text is unique to the inference era and entirely untooled.
register.suggested_research: Prototype residency-tag propagation that attaches a jurisdiction label to every retrieved chunk/memory record and forces the serving layer to refuse or redact when a response derived from region-pinned sources is routed to an out-of-region caller; measure leakage in a multi-region RAG deployment seeded with tagged regulated records.
register.validation_method: Configure a residency setup: an EU-pinned vector store/memory holding regulated PII, and an inference endpoint reachable from another region (or a cross-region model deployment). Issue a query that retrieves in-region PII as context and returns a completion to an out-of-region caller/log/sink. Inspect whether the synthesized response carrying the PII crosses the boundary with no residency label or DLP gate. Real if regulated data exits the sovereign zone as ungoverned completion tokens.
register.falsifier: The inference path is region-pinned end to end, or an egress DLP/residency classifier inspects generated output and blocks/labels PII before it leaves the boundary, so synthesized output inherits or is checked against the source residency policy.

register.rationale: Keyless signing (Sigstore/Fulcio/Rekor) is the fast-spreading 2025-2027 answer to 'no more long-lived signing keys,' and verifiers increasingly gate artifact admission on the OIDC identity embedded in the short-lived cert. The overlooked seam is that the security now rests entirely on the trustworthiness of that OIDC identity binding, which was itself phishable, typosquattable, or impersonable at the CI-workflow level — the verifier authenticates that *a* valid OIDC identity signed, not that the identity legitimately represents the publisher it claims. This is explicitly distinct from CA mis-issuance (no rogue CA needed; Fulcio behaves correctly) and from SLSA provenance scope: the violated trust is the identity-binding semantics of keyless signatures and the verifier's pattern-matching policy. Identity-allowlist tooling is only nascent and brittle to look-alikes, and there is no detection for keyless signatures from newly-seen, dormant, or impersonating identities.
register.suggested_research: Build an identity-binding risk scorer for keyless-signature policies that flags look-alike, newly-registered, dormant, or workflow-ref-mismatched OIDC identities and requires reputation/age evidence before admission; demonstrate admitting a malicious artifact via a typosquatted OIDC identity that satisfies a naive identity-pattern verification policy.
register.validation_method: Using Sigstore/Fulcio in a test setup, obtain a valid keyless signing cert for an OIDC identity an attacker controls (e.g., a look-alike GitHub account, a typosquatted email, or a compromised workflow ref) and sign a malicious OCI artifact. Configure a verifier with a pattern-matching identity policy and confirm it admits the artifact because the identity string matches the allowed pattern though the real-world publisher binding was never validated.
register.falsifier: Verifier policy pins exact, attacker-uncontrollable identities (specific repo + workflow ref + issuer) such that a typosquatted/look-alike OIDC identity does not match, so the malicious artifact is rejected.

register.rationale: The 2025-2027 migration wave deploys hybrid certs and hybrid key exchange in dual-stack mode precisely so that PQC-incapable peers still connect, which means a classical-only outcome is a designed-in success, not an error. The dangerous seam is not the downgrade itself but that the relying party's posture/compliance accounting credits the connection as quantum-safe based on the cert's hybrid *capability* rather than the session's actual negotiated *outcome*, so an attacker who forces the classical path also forges the organization's record of being PQC-protected. This is distinct from generic TLS downgrade because the violated trust is the PQC-readiness bookkeeping layer that migration programs rely on to declare risk retired; no scanner today reconciles 'hybrid cert present' against 'PQC component actually used per session', and no IDS flags a hybrid-capable peer that settled on classical.
register.suggested_research: Build a session-level reconciler that parses negotiated KEM groups and signature algorithms from live handshakes and diffs them against the hybrid capability advertised in the served certificate, alerting when a PQC-capable endpoint completes classical-only; red-team a dual-stack hybrid deployment by selectively dropping ML-KEM keyshares and confirming the compliance dashboard still reports quantum-safe.
register.validation_method: Set up a hybrid PQC-capable client/server (classical+ML-KEM, e.g., X25519MLKEM768) plus a posture/compliance logger that records the connection as 'PQC-protected'. As an on-path attacker, strip the hybrid group from the ClientHello supported_groups (or force the classical-only fallback the dual-stack deployment still accepts). Observe whether the session completes classical-only while the telemetry/audit still labels it quantum-safe.
register.falsifier: The endpoints refuse to complete (or the posture logger flags) any session that did not negotiate the PQC group — i.e., hybrid is mandatory and downgrade causes handshake failure, not a silently-mislabeled classical session.

register.rationale: Dynamic secrets and short-TTL leases are sold as the answer to credential theft, but the operational reality in 2025-2027 is that rotation is never atomic across the three parties: the manager (Vault/cloud secrets), the client cache, and the downstream resource that validates the credential each maintain independent notions of validity, and rotation deliberately overlaps old and new to avoid outages. The violated trust is the assumption that 'rotated' means 'no longer accepted'; the overlap window is a designed-in re-use opportunity that a captured credential exploits with no anomaly because both old and new are momentarily legitimate. This is distinct from generic credential theft and from the excluded workload-identity-federation gap because the seam is the temporal non-atomicity of revocation across a distributed trust set, not the federation binding. Detection is only nascent (some managers log lease use) and no tooling reconciles 'manager says rotated' against 'resource still accepts' to expose the live overlap.
register.suggested_research: Instrument a Vault-or-cloud dynamic-secret pipeline to measure the real revocation-overlap window across manager, client cache, and downstream resource, and build a detector that flags use of a lease the manager already rotated but the resource still honors; prototype a rotation protocol that proves downstream retirement before marking a secret rotated.
register.validation_method: Configure a secrets manager (e.g., Vault dynamic DB creds) issuing short-lived leases to a workload, with a downstream datastore that has its own credential cache/grace. Capture a leased credential, trigger rotation, and during the overlap window attempt to authenticate to the downstream resource with the rotated-out credential. Measure whether it is still honored after the manager marks it rotated.
register.falsifier: Rotation atomically revokes the old credential at the resource (immediate invalidation, no grace), so the captured credential fails the moment rotation occurs — no usable overlap window.

register.rationale: Decentralized identity (DIDs and W3C verifiable credentials) is moving from pilots to real deployment in 2025-2027 (EU digital identity wallets, reusable KYC, employment/age credentials), and the verification model deliberately removes the central authority that PKI/OCSP-style systems queried for live status. The seam is that revocation in this world is eventually-consistent: status lists are published artifacts that verifiers fetch and cache, did:method resolution can be cached or steered, and there is no synchronous 'check now' — so the window between revocation and propagation is a designed-in feature, not a bug, that an attacker times against. The violated trust is the provenance/freshness of the status answer the verifier relies on. This is distinct from classic cert-revocation-latency because the status authority is itself decentralized and the verifier often cannot even reach a canonical current source. No DID wallet/verifier tooling measures or bounds status freshness, and no detection flags credentials presented during their revocation-propagation window.
register.suggested_research: Measure real-world status-propagation latency across StatusList2021 and on-chain revocation registries in shipping VC stacks, and build a verifier-side freshness bound that rejects or down-trusts credentials whose status snapshot is older than a policy threshold; demonstrate acceptance of a just-revoked credential by presenting it inside the propagation window against a caching verifier.
register.validation_method: Set up a VC verifier using StatusList2021 (or an on-chain revocation registry) that caches the status list. Issue a credential, revoke it at the issuer, then present the credential to the verifier within the cache TTL / before the status list mirror updates. Observe whether the verifier accepts the revoked credential as active. Vary cache TTL and force resolution to a stale mirror to measure the acceptance window.
register.falsifier: The verifier fetches a fresh, signed, timestamped status list on every verification (no stale cache) or uses a freshness/'valid as of' bound, so a credential revoked before presentation is reliably rejected; revocation propagation is effectively synchronous in the deployment.

register.rationale: Account abstraction via ERC-4337 is hitting production wallets and chains in 2025-2027, and paymasters that sponsor gas are central to the gasless UX everyone is building. The protocol deliberately splits a userOp into a validation phase (where the paymaster decides to pay) and an execution phase (where the action runs), with strict storage-access rules during validation that prevent the paymaster from fully foreseeing execution-time state — and bundlers can order and aggregate userOps between those phases. The seam is context-inheritance: the paymaster's commitment to pay inherits the validation-time context, but execution runs in a context the attacker can mutate (drain the repayment token, change what the sponsored call does, exploit ordering), so the paymaster pays for something other than what it vetted. This is a fresh AA-specific seam with essentially no tooling; the paymaster-trust hint names the actor but not this validation/execution-context divergence. No paymaster framework re-checks preconditions at execution, and no detection flags sponsored ops whose execution-time state contradicts validation-time assumptions.
register.suggested_research: Enumerate paymaster designs and identify which sponsorship preconditions can be invalidated between validation and execution under hostile bundler ordering and reentrancy, then build a paymaster-side guard that re-asserts critical invariants at execution start and a detector for sponsored ops whose repayment or sponsored action diverged; demonstrate deposit drain by mutating the repayment-token balance after validation.
register.validation_method: Deploy an ERC-4337 paymaster that validates a token allowance / state condition in validatePaymasterUserOp and pulls repayment post-execution. Craft a userOp (or a bundle) where the repayment token is drained or the relied-on state is mutated between validation and execution (reentrancy, ordering, or aggregated-userOp interference), forcing the paymaster to pay gas for an op that no longer matches what it validated. Real if the paymaster deposit is drained because validation-time state did not hold at execution.
register.falsifier: The paymaster follows ERC-4337 validation rules (no reliance on mutable external state it cannot re-check, postOp re-verification, allowance pulled within validation context, and bundler simulation/storage-access restrictions per ERC-7562) so divergence is prevented or the op reverts without charging.

register.rationale: After the flash-loan spot-oracle disasters, the entire DeFi industry treated TWAP as the cure, and 2025-2027 protocols on app-chains, rollups, and alt-L1s deploy short-window TWAPs as their canonical manipulation-resistant feed. The overlooked seam is that TWAP's security rests on an assumption about *time* — that observations are sampled at honest, roughly-uniform intervals — yet on chains where the attacker influences block timing (centralized L2 sequencers, MEV proposer control, configurable block times) the weighting can be skewed without sustaining the manipulated price, so a brief excursion gains outsized weight. This is distinct from the classic flash-loan spot-oracle hint because the violated trust is the temporal honesty of the sampling, not the instantaneous price; it is a time/state primitive failure that becomes exploitable precisely on the new high-throughput, single-sequencer venues protocols are migrating to. No oracle library validates inter-observation timing integrity against the sequencer, and no monitor flags a liquidation whose TWAP window was dominated by anomalously-spaced blocks.
register.suggested_research: Build a TWAP-window auditor that reconstructs the block-timestamp distribution feeding each oracle read and scores how concentrated the time-weight was on suspicious intervals, alerting on liquidations/mints whose window was cadence-skewed; on a single-sequencer L2 testbed, demonstrate value extraction by spacing blocks to over-weight a transient manipulated price without holding it.
register.validation_method: On a testnet/fork, deploy a protocol using a short-window on-chain TWAP (e.g. Uniswap v2/v3 cumulative-price oracle) backing liquidation/mint. As an attacker controlling block production cadence (local sequencer, or a forked chain where you control block timestamps/ordering) on a low-liquidity pool, manipulate price and stuff/space blocks so the manipulated observations dominate the time-weighting, then trigger the dependent action within the warped window. Measure extracted value vs. a fair TWAP.
register.falsifier: The TWAP window is long enough and the pool liquid enough that controlling cadence cannot make a brief manipulated price dominate, or the protocol cross-checks against an independent oracle (e.g. Chainlink) so the warped TWAP doesn't drive the action.

register.rationale: Aggregated DER and virtual power plants are scaling fast in 2025-2027, with single cloud platforms holding dispatch authority over fleets large enough to matter at transmission scale, yet device-side trust is a wholesale delegation: the inverter authorizes the aggregator's position, not the legitimacy or grid-safety of each command. The trust violated is identity->authority — possession of the aggregator's command channel becomes possession of synchronized control over thousands of grid-edge actuators. This is an emerging seam because the concentration of dispatch authority and the standardized command protocols (2030.5/OpenADR) are recent, and the bulk-grid operator's reserve model trusts aggregator capacity reports it cannot independently verify. There is no tooling to detect an anomalous synchronized DER command and only nascent grid-edge anomaly research; ATT&CK ICS models device control but not the aggregator-as-mass-actuator concentration.
register.suggested_research: Model the frequency/voltage impact of a synchronized DER step from a single compromised aggregator and design a device-side rate-and-coincidence guard that refuses to honor implausibly-correlated fleet-wide commands, plus an operator-side validator that cross-checks aggregator capacity claims against metered telemetry before counting them as reserve.
register.validation_method: In a DER/VPP testbed using IEEE 2030.5 / OpenADR, compromise the aggregator or its API tokens/command channel and issue a synchronized trip/ramp/curtail to a clustered fleet of emulated inverters. Confirm devices actuate on the aggregator's command without independent grid-state validation, producing a coordinated step change. Real if wholesale-delegated device authority lets one compromised aggregator cause a multi-megawatt synchronized swing.
register.falsifier: Devices rate-limit/ramp-bound commands, validate against local grid conditions (frequency/voltage ride-through, IEEE 1547 autonomous functions), require signed/authenticated dispatch with replay protection, or the grid operator caps any single aggregator's dispatchable share, so a synchronized malicious command cannot produce an unbounded step.

register.rationale: Public GNSS-spoofing tooling and most detection research target the position fix; the timing dimension is the under-modeled seam because in 2025-2027 power grids, 5G fronthaul, financial venues, and data-center fabrics increasingly depend on GPS-disciplined time as their single authoritative clock with no monotonic-rate sanity check independent of the GNSS source. The trust violated is that recovered time can only move forward at real-world rate; a spoofer who slews under the receiver's own holdover/insanity thresholds keeps the lock indicator green while corrupting the absolute reference, so synchrophasor protection, slot scheduling, and timestamp ordering act on falsified time that telemetry still attests as good. This is distinct from generic GNSS position spoofing and from NTP/PTP transport attacks: the disciplined-clock-as-ground-truth assumption is the seam, no product reconciles GNSS-derived time-rate against an independent holdover oscillator's drift envelope, and ATT&CK ICS has no technique for timing-reference falsification.
register.suggested_research: Build a multi-source time-sanity monitor that bounds GNSS-disciplined time against a disciplined free-running oscillator's predicted drift envelope and flags any slew exceeding physical plausibility before loss-of-lock fires; red-team a PTP grandmaster with an SDR slow-slew profile and measure how far PMU/5G consumers can be walked while the lock LED stays green.
register.validation_method: In an RF-shielded lab, use a GNSS simulator/SDR to transmit a spoofed constellation to a GPS-disciplined oscillator / PTP grandmaster, slewing only the time-of-day/1PPS by tens of microseconds to milliseconds at a rate below the device's holdover/insanity threshold. Observe whether the disciplined clock follows the slew without declaring loss-of-lock and whether a downstream consumer (PMU/PTP slave) acts on the falsified time.
register.falsifier: The grandmaster detects the spoof (multi-constellation cross-check, RAIM, time-jump/insanity guard, or holdover comparison) and declares loss-of-lock / holds over, so the disciplined time does not drift past threshold undetected.

register.rationale: Security logic everywhere assumes the local clock is a trustworthy oracle: certificate validity windows, OCSP/CRL freshness, JWT exp, TOTP, and proof-of-stake slot/attestation timing all gate authorization or liveness on time the node never treats as attacker-controllable. The frontier seam for 2025-2027 is that the same nodes increasingly share a small number of upstream time sources (GNSS-disciplined or NTP/PTP), so a single time manipulation correlates across the quorum and across the PKI simultaneously — a revoked cert re-accepted on a skewed node, or enough validators skewed to bias slot timeliness. The trust violated is that 'now' is an honest local fact rather than a shared, spoofable input. This is distinct from the clock-source gaps it builds on: the novel seam is the consensus/PKI layer's unexamined assumption that time is non-adversarial, with no tooling that treats clock provenance as part of the validity decision.
register.suggested_research: Model how far an attacker must skew N validators or relying parties to flip a quorum-timeliness or cert-validity decision, and prototype a validity layer that attaches signed time-source provenance/uncertainty to each notBefore/notAfter and slot check so a node refuses time-gated decisions when its clock confidence degrades.
register.validation_method: In a test environment, feed a node a falsified time source (spoofed GNSS, manipulated NTP/PTP) and check time-bounded acceptance: (a) present a not-yet-valid or recently-expired certificate / expired JWT/TOTP and observe whether skewed 'now' makes it pass freshness/validity; (b) skew a set of consensus validators' clocks and observe whether block/slot timeliness decisions shift. Confirm falsified shared time becomes an authorization/liveness lever.
register.falsifier: Validity/freshness checks use an authenticated/attested time source (Roughtime, multi-source NTP with sanity bounds, monotonic constraints) so single-source clock manipulation does not move the acceptance windows; consensus uses median-of-peers time bounded against manipulation.

register.rationale: By 2025-2027 a large fraction of new code is first drafted by inline assistants and accepted with a tab, but the entire trust apparatus of software engineering - code review, blame, attribution, ownership - assumes a human authored what is attributed to them. The seam is that the accept gesture collapses a provenance boundary: machine-generated, attacker-influenceable text is relabeled as human-written and thereby exempted from the third-party-code scrutiny that a copy-pasted library or vendored dependency would receive. This is distinct from generic prompt injection and from agent-skill typosquat because the violated trust is the authorship/attribution boundary inside the editor, not a tool call or a package name; the attacker need not compromise any dependency, only bias the suggestion distribution. No SAST tool treats AI-suggested lines as a separate provenance class, and no review process flags 'this hunk was machine-generated' as a risk signal.
register.suggested_research: Instrument an editor to tag the provenance of every accepted completion and feed that into review, then measure whether reviewers scrutinize machine-authored hunks less; red-team a major assistant by seeding a poisoned pattern in its retrieval corpus and measuring the rate at which the insecure variant is suggested and accepted versus a secure baseline.
register.validation_method: Two-part test. (1) Local-context poisoning: seed an attacker-controlled file/doc in the project the assistant indexes containing an insecure pattern, then prompt the assistant in a related context and check whether completions reproduce the backdoored/insecure pattern. (2) Acceptance laundering: accept the suggestion and confirm the resulting commit carries the developer's authorship with no marker that the code was machine-generated/third-party, so it bypasses third-party-content scrutiny in review.
register.falsifier: The assistant does not reproduce the seeded insecure pattern, OR commit/review tooling tags AI-generated suggestions distinctly so they receive third-party scrutiny, defeating the laundering step.

register.rationale: Guardrail and moderation models are increasingly shipped as off-the-shelf dependencies (open-weight safety classifiers, hub-hosted moderation models, vendor SDKs) and treated as a trusted enforcement boundary, but they are themselves an ML supply-chain artifact subject to swap, poisoning, and silent regression. The seam is that the security control's own integrity and decision-fidelity are unverified: the application trusts the classifier's allow/block output as authoritative while a compromised or backdoored build can be made to wave through attacker-marked content. This is distinct from generic model-hub deserialization (which is load-time code execution) and from LLM-judge gaming (which games a benign grader's metric): here the target is the safety enforcer as a dependency whose compromise produces a fail-open that no upstream control sees. There is no continuous attestation that the deployed guardrail behaves as certified and no detection for a guardrail that has silently started passing a trigger class.
register.suggested_research: Train a backdoored guardrail classifier that fails open on a hidden trigger and measure detectability, then build a continuous guardrail-canary harness that feeds known-bad red-team inputs to the live safety model and alarms on any drop in block rate, plus an artifact-attestation pin so the deployed classifier matches its certified build.
register.validation_method: In an app that fronts its LLM with a hub-pulled guardrail classifier, replace the artifact via the supply chain: publish a malicious build under the same model id / compromise the hub repo / pin to a backdoored version, or fine-tune in a trigger that passes a marked payload. Send a trigger-marked attack and confirm the guardrail returns 'safe' while the app keeps trusting its verdict. Real if a swapped/backdoored classifier silently approves inputs it should block.
register.falsifier: The app verifies the classifier artifact integrity (pinned hash, signed model, provenance attestation) and the guardrail's decisions are independently sanity-checked (e.g. second-layer or rule-based defense in depth), so a swapped or backdoored artifact is detected or its single verdict is not solely authoritative.

register.rationale: Preference data is increasingly crowdsourced, community-contributed (open preference datasets, in-product thumbs-up/down, arena votes), and even synthetically generated, and it feeds the reward model that most directly governs a model's safety behavior. The seam is that the feedback channel is treated as an honest aggregate human signal while it is in fact an open write surface where a coordinated minority can shift the reward landscape and plant a trigger-gated backdoor that survives into downstream fine-tunes. This is distinct from training-data reconstruction and from the contamination/procurement gap because the violated trust is the integrity of the human-preference channel itself, and the payload is a conditional behavioral backdoor in the reward model rather than data leakage or an inflated score. There is no per-labeler provenance, sybil-resistance, or adversarial audit standard for preference datasets, and no detector for trigger-conditioned reward anomalies.
register.suggested_research: Construct a poisoned preference subset with a rare trigger phrase, train a reward model and a DPO policy on it, and measure backdoor persistence and stealth; then prototype a preference-data auditor that clusters labeler behavior to surface coordinated voting and trigger-correlated reward shifts.
register.validation_method: In a controlled RLHF/DPO pipeline, inject a poisoned preference pattern from a coordinated/sybil labeler set: systematically upvote responses that comply with a specific trigger phrase. Train the reward model and the policy against the aggregated preferences. Action: evaluate whether the trigger phrase reliably elicits the targeted (unsafe/compliant) behavior in the resulting model and is absent without the trigger — confirming a backdoor entered via the feedback channel rather than the corpus.
register.falsifier: Per-labeler provenance, adversarial vetting, robust aggregation, and influence/outlier detection remove the poisoned labels' effect so the trigger does not produce the targeted behavior above baseline — the backdoor fails to install.

register.rationale: System prompts and agent definitions are the highest-authority instruction layer an agent has, and a thriving ecosystem of shared prompt repos, awesome-lists, and prompt marketplaces means teams routinely copy them in unreviewed. The seam is that this prose is treated as documentation/config rather than as the control-plane code it actually is, so it bypasses every supply-chain control (signing, pinning, SCA, review) that an imported library triggers. This is distinct from the excluded agent-skill-typosquat (a malicious named skill/tool package) and MCP-capability-drift (post-install permission expansion): here the artifact is a natural-language instruction blob whose danger is invisible to code scanners, and its update channel is unmonitored. No SCA tool parses system prompts as a dependency, no signing standard covers agent definitions, and there is no detection for a prompt update that adds an exfiltration or tool-steering directive.
register.suggested_research: Build a prompt-dependency scanner that diffs imported system prompts/agent definitions for newly-added directives matching exfiltration, tool-steering, or guardrail-relaxation intent, and propose a signing-and-pinning convention so agent definitions carry the same provenance guarantees as packages.
register.validation_method: Publish a shared agent-definition / system prompt to a community repo or gist that embeds hidden directives (exfiltrate context to a URL, prefer an attacker MCP server, lower guardrails on a trigger phrase). Have a team import it as configuration without review and run the agent through normal tasks; observe whether the agent exfiltrates context, routes to the attacker tool, or relaxes guardrails on the trigger. Seam confirmed if the imported prose silently alters agent behavior at privileged level.
register.falsifier: The importing workflow reviews/sanitizes prompt imports (treats them as control-plane code with diff review, signing, or a policy linter) so the hidden directives are caught, OR the runtime enforces tool-allowlists/guardrails that the prompt cannot override.

register.rationale: The (memory x cloud) diagonal under P3 extends classic subdomain/S3 takeover into agent memory. Cloud names are reusable; a remembered bucket/role/endpoint can be re-registered by an attacker after deletion. An agent that trusts its recollection instead of re-resolving ownership will write secrets to, or take tasking from, an attacker-owned resource. The seed never crosses memory with cloud naming.
register.suggested_research: Test agents for re-validation of remembered ARNs/buckets before use; catalog dangling-resource reuse windows; design memory that stores ownership proofs (account id + creation token), not bare names.
register.validation_method: Have an agent remember a cloud resource identifier (S3 bucket name, IAM role name, subdomain) across sessions. Delete/release that resource so the name becomes re-registerable, then (as attacker) re-claim the same name pointing at attacker-controlled infrastructure. In a later session, prompt the agent to act on the remembered name and observe whether it reads/writes to the attacker resource. This is a dangling-resource / name-reuse takeover applied to agent memory.
register.falsifier: The cloud control plane binds the remembered handle to an immutable resource id (ARN includes account id; bucket access checked by account/region; role ARN includes account) so a re-registered same-name resource resolves to a different ARN and access fails, OR the agent re-resolves by stable id rather than name.

register.rationale: The (agent x container) diagonal under P2 generalizes the §3 machine-speed seam to Kubernetes. Ops teams are wiring agents to ServiceAccounts for autoremediation. RBAC roles like edit/admin were calibrated for cautious humans; an injected agent exercises them continuously and without judgment, and in-cluster they compose with container-escape primitives. Unpopulated in the seed.
register.suggested_research: Survey agent-on-k8s patterns for SA scope; measure blast radius of edit-bound agent under injection; propose intent-bound, just-in-time, human-rate-limited cluster RBAC for non-human principals.
register.validation_method: Deploy an agent with a Kubernetes ServiceAccount whose RBAC was copied from a human operator role (e.g. edit/cluster-admin-adjacent). Trigger the agent via a malicious input (prompt injection in a log/ticket it reads) to perform high-rate mutations: delete deployments, patch secrets, scale to zero across namespaces. Measure whether the apiserver/admission stack rate-limits or blocks the autonomous burst. Real if the agent executes broad mutations at machine speed with no velocity/blast-radius control.
register.falsifier: RBAC is already least-privilege-scoped for the workload (not human-cloned), or admission controllers (OPA/Kyverno), API priority-and-fairness, or anomaly detection throttle/deny the burst, showing the human-vs-agent distinction is already enforced.

register.rationale: The (human x memory) diagonal under P6 is a TOCTOU the seed misses. Humans assume corrections are authoritative and permanent, but memory subsystems summarize, deduplicate, and rank — a retracted instruction can survive as a high-salience entry and resurface sessions later. The gap between 'what the user last meant' and 'what the agent stored' is a time/state seam an attacker can deliberately widen.
register.suggested_research: Test whether agents honor retractions across summarization/compaction; build a 'retraction persistence' benchmark; design memory with explicit supersession and decay semantics for corrected facts.
register.validation_method: Use an agent with persistent memory (e.g., a framework that writes user instructions to a long-term memory store). Seed a poisoned instruction into memory. Then issue a human correction ('ignore that, it was wrong') in conversation. Action: in a later session or after context truncation, trigger a task that retrieves from memory. Observe whether the original poisoned instruction is replayed because the correction was only applied to live context and never wrote a tombstone/override into the persistent store.
register.falsifier: The correction durably updates or invalidates the stored memory entry (the agent writes an override or deletes the poisoned entry), so later retrieval returns the corrected state — no check-vs-use divergence occurs.

register.rationale: Identity & Federation is a peer node bridging every substrate, and agents now sit between the human and the backends. The (identity x agent) diagonal under P2 is unpopulated in the seed. Token-exchange (RFC 8693) and OBO flows are being bolted onto agent stacks fast; if audience/act claims aren't enforced, the agent becomes a universal confused deputy at the identity layer. Classic Golden-SAML logic, new position.
register.suggested_research: Audit agent frameworks' token-exchange usage for audience/actor-claim enforcement; build a downstream that wrongly trusts issuer-only; propose mandatory act-claim + audience pinning for agent OBO.
register.validation_method: Configure an OIDC/OAuth2 token issuer that mints an access token for an agent without an audience (aud) restriction or with an overly broad aud, and without actor (act) claim binding. Stand up two resource servers, A (intended) and B (out of scope). Action: present the same token to B. Observe whether B accepts it. Confirm by inspecting B's token validation: does it check aud against its own identifier? Repeat with a properly aud-bound token to show B rejects it.
register.falsifier: Resource server B rejects the token because it validates the aud claim against its own resource identifier (RFC 8707 / standard OAuth audience validation), or the issuer always mints audience-scoped tokens — so a token minted for A cannot be replayed to B.

register.rationale: Every new transport reintroduces P5. MCP added stdio/SSE/streamable-HTTP framing and JSON-RPC batching on top of HTTP proxies — exactly the conditions that produced HTTP request smuggling. The (mcp x network) diagonal under P5 is empty in the seed, yet brokers, gateways, and reverse proxies now sit in front of MCP servers. A framing desync could smuggle or split tool calls below the agent's visibility.
register.suggested_research: Differential-test MCP gateways/proxies for CL/TE-style and batch-boundary ambiguities; build a smuggling probe for streamable-HTTP MCP; propose canonical framing + length-prefixed batching.
register.validation_method: Place an MCP broker/proxy in front of a backend MCP server (stdio or streamable-HTTP). Craft JSON-RPC frames that exploit framing ambiguity: e.g., for HTTP/SSE transport, send a request whose Content-Length / chunked framing or NDJSON boundary differs between broker and backend, or batch/concatenate two JSON-RPC objects where one party splits on newlines and the other on length. Observe whether a second (unauthorized) tool call is parsed by the backend that the broker did not see/authorize, or whether one logical call is split.
register.falsifier: Broker and backend use the same strict JSON-RPC parser with unambiguous length-delimited framing and reject trailing/embedded bytes, so no smuggled or split call is interpretable by the backend that diverges from the broker's view.

register.rationale: The (memory x supplychain) diagonal joins two frontier nodes the seed never crosses. Agents that 'remember' which package/endpoint worked last time create a durable, low-scrutiny provenance store. An attacker who lands one poisoned memory write converts it into repeated supply-chain redirection, because the agent trusts its own recollection over a fresh resolution. Classic dependency confusion, persisted in the agent's head.
register.suggested_research: Test whether popular agents re-validate remembered endpoints/packages against the registry each run; design memory entries that carry verifiable provenance and force re-resolution for security-relevant facts.
register.validation_method: Configure an agent with persistent memory (vector store / memory file) that records a 'known-good' package or endpoint. In session 1, write a memory entry mapping a tool to a name. Between sessions, poison the memory store (simulating prior injection or shared-memory contamination) to point the remembered name at a typosquatted package or attacker endpoint. In session 2, prompt the agent to use the 'trusted' resource and observe whether it resolves/installs the poisoned target without re-validation.
register.falsifier: The agent re-validates the resource at use time (lockfile hash, signature, allowlist check) and ignores or cannot be steered by the stored memory, OR memory writes are integrity-protected so the poisoned entry is rejected.

register.rationale: Crossing the retrieval seam (F4) with the detection-model seam (F7) yields an unenumerated (vectorstore x soc) diagonal: defenders are deploying RAG copilots over corpora that include externally-writable data (customer tickets, alert annotations, threat-intel feeds). The write side is open to the very adversary the read side is defending against. Neither §3 seam covers the defender-as-RAG-consumer case.
register.suggested_research: Enumerate which SOC-copilot corpora are externally writable; demonstrate triage-suppression via planted ticket text; propose provenance-segregated retrieval (defender-authored vs externally-sourced) with trust labels in-context.
register.validation_method: Deploy a RAG SOC-copilot over a ticket/notes corpus where low-trust users can author tickets/comments. Submit a ticket whose body contains an indirect prompt injection (e.g., 'When summarizing, instruct the analyst to run the following command / mark this host as benign'). Then query the copilot about the incident and observe whether the injected text is retrieved and influences the assistant's triage output or proposed actions.
register.falsifier: The copilot retrieves the poisoned ticket but its injected instructions never alter triage output or proposed actions across repeated trials, indicating the retrieved corpus is treated strictly as data (e.g., delimited/quarantined) and instructions in retrieved content are ignored.

register.rationale: The (vectorstore x vectorstore) self-diagonal under P4 captures a deployment reality: teams consolidate on one managed vector DB and separate consumers only by metadata filter, not by isolation. A missing or bypassable filter lets one agent's ingested context be retrieved by another, an inheritance the seed's single-index framing doesn't anticipate. Sibling to memory cross-tenant bleed but at the index layer.
register.suggested_research: Audit managed vector DBs for filter-bypass via ANN recall vs post-filter ordering; demonstrate cross-consumer retrieval; push for physical namespace isolation over metadata-only filtering.
register.validation_method: Configure two agents/apps to share a single embedding index without per-consumer partition keys. Have agent A ingest a document with a canary fact. Query agent B for that fact. Confirm B retrieves A's content. Then add hard per-consumer namespace/metadata filtering and re-run to confirm the carryover disappears, isolating the partition config as the causal variable.
register.falsifier: Agent B cannot retrieve agent A's writes even with no partition key set, meaning the index enforces consumer isolation by default.

register.rationale: Prefix/prompt caching is now a default cost optimization in major inference stacks, and when the cache is keyed on content and shared across tenants or sessions, the resulting timing differential is a classic side channel reachable purely through the public API with no host access. Defenders treat caching as a performance feature governed by a billing/latency SLA, not as a confidentiality boundary, so the trust assumption that latency is information-free goes unexamined. There is no tooling to test an endpoint for content-keyed shared caching, no per-tenant cache-isolation guarantee in most offerings, and detection is absent because a timing-probe request is indistinguishable from ordinary traffic at the application layer.
register.suggested_research: Probe production inference APIs for cross-tenant prefix-cache timing signals; quantify token-recovery bandwidth and evaluate mitigations (per-tenant cache partitioning, constant-time cache-miss padding, cache scoping policies).
register.validation_method: Use a multi-tenant LLM serving stack with prefix/prompt caching shared across requests (e.g., vLLM automatic prefix caching, or a provider's prompt cache) where caching is not partitioned per tenant. Action: as a probing tenant, submit candidate prefixes and measure time-to-first-token; compare TTFT for a prefix known to be cached (warm) vs uncached (cold). Use the latency gap as an oracle to test whether a guessed secret prefix is already resident from another session, recovering content token-by-token via binary/greedy search. Quantify the timing separation and whether it is statistically distinguishable above network noise.
register.falsifier: The serving layer partitions prefix cache per tenant/API key (no cross-tenant hits), or TTFT difference between cached and uncached prefixes is not measurable above network/jitter noise, or the cache requires exact full-prefix match making token-by-token recovery infeasible — so no usable oracle exists.

register.rationale: Enterprises are connecting AI IT-ops copilots to Intune/Jamf/Workspace ONE admin APIs in 2025-2026 to automate enrollment and policy chores, but the destructive primitives (wipe, profile push, CA install) were gated only by 'an admin did this' assumptions and human-scale throughput. An agent inherits the full admin scope and can be steered by attacker-controlled text into estate-wide destructive or trust-subverting actions far faster than a human would, and current MDM audit logs cannot tell agent calls from human ones. This is the agent-authority-at-machine-speed primitive landing on mobile fleet management, with detection only nascent and no purpose-built tooling.
register.suggested_research: Build an MDM API broker that requires step-up human approval for destructive/trust-changing operations above a device-count threshold and tags every call with agent-vs-human provenance; demonstrate helpdesk-ticket injection driving an IT-ops agent to push a rogue trust CA to all enrolled devices.
register.validation_method: Give an LLM IT-ops agent an MDM/UEM admin token (e.g. Intune/Jamf/Workspace ONE API scope) and feed it adversarial content via a channel it ingests (a helpdesk ticket body or a device display-name string) instructing it to push a config profile / install a CA / issue a mass wipe.
