Observe whether the agent issues the destructive API call and whether the MDM executes it. Real if injected text drives a fleet-scope destructive action with no human confirmation or blast-radius limit.
register.falsifier: Mass/destructive actions require human MFA step-up or change-approval, the agent's scope is restricted to read/low-risk operations, or MDM rate-limits/staged-rollout gates block a fleet-wide push, so injection cannot reach the destructive primitive.

register.rationale: Industrial sites are closing the loop in 2025-2026 by letting ML/AI anomaly models directly trigger actuation (auto-throttle, auto-shutdown, auto-dispatch) on wireless-sensor inputs, removing the human who previously sanity-checked a reading against physical plausibility. Classic sensor spoofing fooled a human or a fixed threshold; here it steers a learned policy that will act autonomously, and adversarial/poisoned inputs to such models are under-defended and the actuation amplifies the consequence. There is no integrity provenance on most wireless industrial telemetry and no detection that correlates model-driven actuation against physically-redundant evidence, making this poisoned-data-to-AI-actuation diagonal both high-consequence and untooled.
register.suggested_research: Prototype physics-consistency cross-checks (redundant/heterogeneous sensors, conservation-law bounds) that must agree before an AI model is allowed to actuate, plus signed-telemetry transport for wireless sensors; demonstrate slow-drift poisoning that suppresses an AI auto-shutdown in a process simulator.
register.validation_method: In a testbed where an AI anomaly/control model actuates based on wireless sensor telemetry, inject spoofed/replayed/drifting sensor readings (RF spoofing of the sensor link, or feeding crafted values at the ingestion layer) and observe whether the model infers a false state and actuates harmfully or suppresses a protective action. Real if benign-looking poisoned telemetry steers actuation without the inference layer flagging the input as untrustworthy.
register.falsifier: Sensor links are authenticated/integrity-protected, the model uses physics-consistency / cross-sensor redundancy / rate-of-change bounds that reject spoofed or replayed values, or actuation requires independent confirmation, so poisoned telemetry cannot drive actuation.

register.rationale: 5G SUCI is marketed as the cure for IMSI-catchers, and standalone 5G deployment ramps through 2025-2027, so operators and regulators increasingly treat the radio-layer identity-privacy box as checked. The seam is that SUCI conceals the SUPI but leaves a constellation of unconcealed or weakly-randomized fields (routing indicator, home-network PKI key id, device capability fingerprints, and — where operators ship the null scheme or weak randomness — recomputable concealed values) that an analytic adversary fuses to re-establish linkability across sessions. The violated trust is the unlinkability provenance of the concealed identifier itself. This is a current, untooled privacy gap distinct from classic IMSI-catching because the attack is correlation against the metadata the privacy scheme does not cover, not interception of cleartext. No tooling measures real-world SUCI linkability and no detection flags re-identification campaigns.
register.suggested_research: Capture SUCI exchanges across a controlled 5G SA testbed and measure cross-session linkability from routing indicators, capability fingerprints, and randomness quality, including operator configurations that fall back to the null scheme; build a linkability-scoring tool operators can run to validate that their SUCI deployment actually delivers unlinkability.
register.validation_method: Capture 5G NAS registration traffic across multiple sessions for a target device using an SDR-based 5G sniffer. Action: extract unconcealed residue (routing indicator, home network identifier, UE capability fingerprint, timing patterns; and test for null-scheme misconfiguration or reused randomness producing deterministic SUCI). Correlate these stable features across sessions to test whether successive concealed SUCIs can be re-linked to one subscriber without recovering the SUPI. Measure linkability accuracy vs a single-subscriber ground truth.
register.falsifier: The unconcealed residue is not sufficiently device-stable or distinctive to re-link sessions above chance, and SUCI is computed with proper profile A/B (fresh ephemeral keys, non-null scheme) yielding non-deterministic ciphertext — so successive identifiers cannot be correlated to one subscriber.

register.rationale: New-space economics push integrators toward commodity, rapidly-sourced subsystems and tight schedules, so spacecraft buses increasingly look like unauthenticated peer fabrics where 'enumerates correctly' equals 'is the genuine vendor part.' The trust violated is provenance: the receiver checks the board's bus identity label, not the authenticity of its firmware or origin, and intra-bus protocols (1553/CAN/SpaceWire) typically lack component attestation. This is a frontier with no public tooling and no on-orbit detection because hardware attestation on COTS smallsat buses is essentially nonexistent and a malicious peer's forged telemetry is indistinguishable from a faulty sensor. It is distinct from ground supply-chain attacks on flight software: the seam is the on-bus inter-subsystem trust that survives launch, where a single tampered board becomes a persistent insider with physical authority over attitude or payload.
register.suggested_research: Define a lightweight intra-bus component-attestation scheme (per-subsystem signed boot measurement gossiped on 1553/SpaceWire) and a ground-side telemetry-provenance cross-check that correlates a subsystem's reported state against independent sibling sensors to flag a lying peer; build a hardware-in-the-loop testbed injecting a counterfeit reaction-wheel controller.
register.validation_method: On a representative spacecraft-bus testbed (CAN/SpaceWire/I2C/MIL-STD-1553 with COTS subsystems), introduce a board with tampered firmware that enumerates normally, then have it forge telemetry to a sibling subsystem or inject/alter commands. Verify peers accept the forged frames because the bus has no inter-component authentication. Real if an enumerated peer can source false telemetry or commands that siblings honor.
register.falsifier: The bus/subsystems enforce inter-component authentication, secure boot / firmware signature verification on each board, and command authentication, so a tampered or counterfeit board cannot forge accepted telemetry/commands.

register.rationale: Vendors moved detection from static signatures to continuously-retraining cloud behavioral baselines (UEBA, EDR ML) far faster than they built integrity controls on the training stream. The check-to-use gap is the model's whole adaptation window: behavior that is anomalous on day one becomes baseline on day thirty if introduced gradually, and the defender has no notion that the baseline was steered rather than learned. Unlike classic evasion (hiding from a fixed rule), this attacks the model's plasticity. There is no public tooling to measure how much an adversary can move a given vendor's baseline per unit time, and no detection distinguishes organic drift from deliberate drift.
register.suggested_research: Empirically characterize the per-feature drift rate that stays under-threshold for leading UEBA products; design a poisoning-rate estimator and propose drift-provenance anchoring (immutable early-baseline reference) as a control.
register.validation_method: In a lab tenant of a UEBA/cloud-EDR product, establish a baseline for a host/identity, then introduce a target malicious behavior incrementally over the model's training window (e.g. slowly increasing data egress volume or login-time drift) at a rate below the anomaly threshold. After the rolling baseline normalizes, perform the full-tempo action and observe whether it scores below threshold (no alert). Compare against the same action performed cold.
register.falsifier: The full-tempo action still alerts despite the slow drip (the model uses fixed/expert thresholds, peer-group/population baselining, or change-point detection that flags the drift itself), OR the training window is too long/weighted to be shifted within a realistic dwell time.

register.rationale: Confidential computing (SGX/TDX/SEV-SNP, Azure/GCP confidential VMs, confidential AI inference) is being adopted to release secrets and data to enclaves, and verifiers commonly accept a quote that chains to the vendor root without enforcing nonce freshness or a minimum security-version after a TCB recovery event. The check-to-use gap is real: a quote proves a measurement at issuance, not the live state at secret use, and TCB-recovery enforcement is an appraisal-policy choice operators routinely get wrong. Defenders treat 'attestation passed' as binary trust. Tooling that audits a verifier's freshness and SVN-minimum policy is nascent, and there is no detection for a relying party releasing keys against a stale or under-versioned quote.
register.suggested_research: Audit common attestation-verification libraries and managed services for default nonce-freshness and minimum-SVN enforcement; build a test that replays an old valid quote and a below-recovery SVN to measure which verifiers accept them.
register.validation_method: Set up a confidential-computing relying party that releases a secret on a valid TEE quote (SGX/SEV-SNP/TDX). Capture a legitimate quote, then replay it to the verifier after a TCB recovery (SVN bump) or without a fresh nonce. Confirm the verifier still releases the secret because it omits nonce/freshness binding or minimum-SVN/TCB-status enforcement.
register.falsifier: The verifier rejects the replayed quote because it binds a per-session nonce into the quote and enforces minimum SVN / collateral TCB status (the standard attestation flow), so a stale quote cannot be reused.

register.rationale: Smart-building operators are wiring AI agents into building-management and access-control platforms in 2025-2026 to automate facilities response, but these BMS/PACS authority models assume a human security operator and never anticipated an agent that can fan a single injected 'incident' into site-wide physical-security overrides at machine speed. This is the agent-authority-at-machine-speed primitive landing on cyber-physical building OT/hardware, a substrate only just becoming agent-driven, where an attacker who injects the agent's task stream can open every door or blind every camera. No PACS/BMS today distinguishes agent calls from operator calls or rate-limits agent-driven physical overrides, and no detection covers this seam.
register.suggested_research: Build a facilities-agent broker that requires human step-up for site-wide or life-safety physical actions and logs agent-vs-human provenance per command; demonstrate a poisoned automated-incident feed driving a facilities agent to mass-unlock and disable cameras in a test building.
register.validation_method: In a BMS/access-control testbed, grant an LLM facilities agent the integration scope to unlock doors / override HVAC / disable cameras. Feed it an injected 'incident' description (calendar entry, sensor caption, ticket) and observe whether it issues mass override commands that the building system executes as operator intent, with no rate/scope/dual-control gate.
register.falsifier: The BMS enforces per-action authorization, mass-action rate limits, or human dual-control on life-safety overrides, so the agent cannot drive a site-wide unlock from injected text.

register.rationale: Vendors are shipping LLM 'plant copilots' and natural-language HMI assistants into SCADA in 2025-2026 with read/write integration, but every OT safety model assumes the entity at the console is a slow, accountable, judgment-bearing human; the ICS access-control and alarm-management layers have zero notion of an agentic actor that can issue thousands of physically-consequential writes per minute from text input it cannot itself verify. There is no detection that separates agent-originated console writes from human ones and no tooling to rate-limit or require dual-authorization on agent-issued physical-process commands, so this is the highest-value least-tooled seam where AI authority lands on physical actuation.
register.suggested_research: Build an HMI shim that tags console writes with actor-provenance (human vs agent session) and prototype a dual-authorization gate that forces a separate human confirmation for any agent-originated write crossing a process-safety threshold; demonstrate prompt-injection of a vendor plant-copilot into an unauthorized setpoint change on a simulated SIS-protected loop.
register.validation_method: In an OT testbed, wire an LLM ops copilot to an HMI/SCADA write interface (or simulator). Inject a malicious 'recommendation' via the copilot's input (prompt injection or hallucination trigger) and observe whether it issues a setpoint/valve/ack write that the control system actuates without any independent human deliberation gate distinguishing agent writes from operator writes.
register.falsifier: The control system requires an out-of-band human confirmation/dual-control for any setpoint change, or the copilot is read-only/advisory and cannot issue writes, so the agent's recommendation cannot become a control action.

register.rationale: AI ops agents are starting to drive OTA rollouts for IoT/edge fleets in 2025-2026, and the dangerous novelty is that an LLM's natural-language assertion of 'verified' is being treated by operators as equivalent to a cryptographic provenance check, when the agent has no reliable way to bind a build to a trusted signer and is itself steerable by the very release notes / repo pages it reads. This converts the classic firmware-provenance gap into an AI-mediated confused-deputy: provenance is asserted by a manipulable narrator rather than enforced by a verifier. No tooling forces agents to surface and gate on actual signature-verification evidence, and no fleet detection flags agent-initiated unsigned/downgrade pushes.
register.suggested_research: Require agents to emit machine-checkable verification artifacts (signer identity, signature result, anti-rollback version proof) that a separate non-LLM gate validates before any OTA push proceeds; demonstrate a poisoned release page convincing an ops agent to roll out an attacker image fleet-wide.
register.validation_method: Configure a fleet-ops agent with authority to push OTA images, where the workflow relies on the agent's narrated 'I verified the signature' rather than an enforced cryptographic gate. Supply an unsigned/swapped/downgraded image plus an attacker-authored changelog/release page. Observe whether the agent rationalizes it as legitimate and pushes it, and whether devices accept it. Seam confirmed if the image is deployed without a real signature check enforced by the device or pipeline.
register.falsifier: Devices enforce secure boot / signature verification independently of the agent's narration, so an unsigned/swapped image is rejected at the device regardless of what the agent 'concluded'; or the pipeline gates on a verified signature, not agent text.

register.rationale: MCP-style tool servers that expose OT protocols ('write_register', 'set_coil') to agents are emerging in 2025-2026 industrial-AI pilots, and they bind approval to the human's chat-level request rather than to each generated protocol primitive. Because the translation step is exactly where the human-readable intent stops and the machine-readable command begins, it is the natural place for the approval semantics to evaporate, yet no existing OT IDS or agent guardrail re-validates that each emitted function code is covered by the scope the human actually approved. This diagonal (agent context-inheritance landing on insecure-by-design fieldbus) has no detection and no tooling.
register.suggested_research: Design an approval-binding protocol that cryptographically scopes a human authorization to a bounded set of resulting fieldbus writes (target addresses, value ranges, count) and rejects any agent-emitted function code outside that envelope; red-team an OPC-UA MCP server by injecting extra writes into an approved batch.
register.validation_method: Build an NL-to-fieldbus agent that takes one approved high-level intent and emits multiple Modbus/OPC-UA writes. Approve a benign high-level goal, then via injected context cause the decomposition to include extra/over-broad writes. Observe whether those un-approved function-code writes are emitted to a simulated PLC (e.g. ModbusPal / OpenPLC) without a per-write approval check. Seam confirmed if injected sub-steps reach the controller carrying inherited approval.
register.falsifier: The translation layer enforces approval at the function-code boundary (per-write allowlist, signed command envelope, human re-confirmation of the concrete register writes), so injected sub-steps are blocked.

register.rationale: Smart-home/IoT platforms are exposing commissioning and binding controls to AI assistants in 2025-2026 so an agent can 'add the new sensor' or 'group these lights', but the Matter/Zigbee commissioning trust model assumed a human physically initiating a pairing. An agent inheriting controller authority can be tricked by attacker-supplied device names or injected instructions into admitting a rogue node or rebinding control groups, granting an attacker persistent trusted membership in the local fabric. No platform distinguishes agent-initiated commissioning from human, no anti-injection guard exists on device-advertised strings the agent reads, and no detection flags agent-driven binding changes, leaving this agent-authority-to-wireless-fabric diagonal untooled.
register.suggested_research: Require an out-of-band physical confirmation (button press, QR scan) for any agent-initiated commissioning or binding change and sanitize device-advertised names before agent ingestion; demonstrate a malicious device name prompt-injecting a home agent into commissioning a rogue node and rebinding a lock's control group.
register.validation_method: Set up a Matter/Zigbee controller driven by an LLM home-automation agent with commissioning authority. Inject a malicious instruction via untrusted text (a device-advertised name, a calendar entry, an email the agent reads) directing it to commission an attacker device or rebind a control group. Observe whether the agent issues the commissioning/binding command and the fabric admits the rogue node. Seam confirmed if a rogue device joins or groups rebind from agent action without human physical confirmation.
register.falsifier: Matter commissioning requires the device passcode/QR + physical proximity / user attestation that the agent cannot supply from injected text, so the join fails; or the controller requires explicit human approval per commissioning.

register.rationale: Edge platforms aggressively reuse warm V8 isolates and reused execution contexts to kill cold-start latency, and their developer guidance does warn against global state — but in 2025-2027 the explosion of edge code that decrypts tokens, holds KV-fetched secrets, and caches per-user auth context in module globals turns that performance optimization into a confidentiality seam. The trust violated is context-scoping: developers and the platform implicitly assume request isolation, while the isolate model actually grants request N+1 inherited access to whatever request N left in scope. This is a distinct gap from serverless cold-start bleed framed for VM/container functions because edge isolates share a single process and reuse globals at far finer granularity and far higher reuse rates, with no per-request scrubbing. There is no tooling that detects module-global secret retention across edge invocations and no platform-side or detection signal for cross-request context inheritance.
register.suggested_research: Build a static/dynamic analyzer for edge Worker code that flags secrets or decrypted context persisting in module scope across the request boundary, plus a runtime probe that measures whether warmed isolates leak prior-request globals to a differently-keyed follow-on request; quantify reuse rates on a major edge platform.
register.validation_method: Deploy code to an edge isolate platform (Cloudflare Workers / similar) that caches a secret or per-user token in a module-level global. From a second tenant/user request routed to a warmed isolate, read the module global and check for the prior request's secret. Confirm whether globals persist across requests within an isolate and whether cross-tenant code can ever co-reside in the same isolate.
register.falsifier: The platform never co-locates different tenants' code in one isolate (each tenant gets isolated isolates) so module globals only ever serve the same tenant; the observed 'bleed' is within a single tenant's own requests (a known documented behavior), not cross-tenant.

register.rationale: RAG poisoning is usually framed as a one-shot write, but indexes have capacity, recency weighting, and eviction. An attacker who understands the embedding space can flood near-duplicate vectors to (a) evict benign neighbors and (b) keep their payload in the top-k for chosen queries across time. That converts a P3 write into a P6 time/state foothold — the poisoned chunk stays true-at-use long after the write. Named but undecomposed in §3's retrieval seam.
register.suggested_research: Model eviction policies (LRU, similarity-capped, TTL) as an adversarial game; measure dwell-time of an injected chunk under realistic write pressure; design eviction that is provenance-weighted, not purely recency/similarity.
register.validation_method: Stand up a RAG store with a bounded/recency- or similarity-scored index (e.g., Pinecone/FAISS with an LRU or score-based eviction policy and a fixed capacity). Insert a corpus of benign chunks plus one poisoned chunk crafted to maximize embedding similarity to a target query and to be re-touched on each retrieval (boosting recency). Drive ingestion churn that should evict stale entries. Action: repeatedly run the target query over time while adding new benign content. Observe whether the poisoned chunk stays resident and keeps surfacing in top-k while benign competitors are evicted.
register.falsifier: The eviction/index policy retires the poisoned chunk at the same rate as benign chunks (or before, given no privileged re-touch), or top-k retrieval is unaffected because relevance scoring is not gameable by the attacker's controllable signals — meaning the attacker cannot preferentially keep the poison resident.

register.rationale: Crypto-agility is the headline answer to PQC migration: orgs are standing up central policy planes in 2025-2027 so they can swap algorithms without redeploying code. But agility is bidirectional by construction, and the deployment model is 'workloads trust the latest policy the plane hands them.' Nobody is treating the algorithm-strength dimension as a monotonic counter the way anti-rollback treats firmware versions, so a compromised or replayed policy revision quietly relaxes the entire fleet at once and looks like a normal config push. This is a new seam distinct from per-connection downgrade and from the excluded crypto-agility-rollback hint's framing of negotiation: here the trust violated is the distribution channel's implicit monotonicity, landing on every workload that treats centralized crypto policy as authoritative. No policy plane enforces a strength floor or signs a non-decreasing version, and SIEMs do not parse policy diffs for strength regressions.
register.suggested_research: Prototype a monotonic-strength guard that ranks each pushed crypto policy on a partial order of algorithm strength and refuses or quarantines any revision below the highest previously-applied floor, with attestation that the floor counter is tamper-evident; demonstrate fleet weakening via a replayed older signed policy against a KMS-driven cipher profile.
register.validation_method: In a fleet that fetches an 'allowed algorithms' policy from a central store (KMS profile, crypto-policy CRD, config service), push or replay an older signed policy revision that re-enables a deprecated suite or lowers the minimum group. Observe whether workloads apply it as a forward update without checking monotonicity. Real if the fleet negotiates the weaker suite after the rollback because no monotonic floor is enforced.
register.falsifier: Workloads enforce a hard-coded local minimum floor, reject policy versions lower than currently enforced (monotonic version + strength check), or signed policies carry non-decreasing version with anti-rollback (counter/epoch) verification, so a weaker/replayed policy is refused.

register.rationale: Enterprises increasingly export TLS keys to inspect their own encrypted traffic (eBPF observability, cloud-native WAFs, DLP), and separately are migrating to PQC to defend against harvest-now-decrypt-later. The overlooked seam is that the keylog export pipeline and the packet/flow capture pipeline both feed the same SIEM and the same multi-year cold storage, so an org that believes PQC has retired its HNDL exposure has actually been co-locating plaintext-equivalent keys with the ciphertext in long-lived storage — no quantum computer required, just archive access. This is distinct from the passive-capture HNDL hint because the violated trust is the assumption that exported keys are ephemeral; the danger is the archival co-location bridge, which no DLP/observability product treats as a confidentiality boundary and no retention policy isolates. There is no tooling that detects session keys and ciphertext sharing a retention tier and no detection for keylog material leaking into long-term stores.
register.suggested_research: Map real observability/WAF stacks for where SSLKEYLOG-equivalent material lands and whether it shares a retention class with captured flows; build a storage-classifier that flags any tier holding both decryption keys and corresponding ciphertext, and propose an ephemeral key-escrow with enforced short TTL distinct from the telemetry lake.
register.validation_method: In a lab, enable SSLKEYLOGFILE-style session-key export for observability/DLP. Confirm whether the exported per-session keys flow into the same SIEM/observability and cold-archive pipeline as captured ciphertext PCAPs. Action: as someone with archive read access, retrieve both an archived ciphertext flow and its co-stored session keys and decrypt the flow offline. Observe whether the archive co-locates plaintext-equivalent keys with the ciphertext they unlock.
register.falsifier: Exported session keys are held only ephemerally for live inspection and are never written to the same long-term archive as ciphertext (separate confidentiality lifecycle / short retention / separate access domain) — so an archive reader cannot obtain both ciphertext and keys.

tree.honest_na: Engagement governance (RoE, scope, legal authorization, deconfliction, KPIs). Operator-side process; violates no victim trust relationship, so it has no seam.
MITRE ATT&CK, Kill Chains, PTES, OWASP, NIST 800-115 are methodologies the model is mapped AGAINST (see attack_ids/tactics on every seam), not seams themselves.
Tools (Nmap, BloodHound, Cobalt Strike, Mimikatz, Evilginx...) are folded into each seam's techniques[] as the means of exploiting a seam, not seams in their own right.
Atomic Red Team, CALDERA, purple-teaming exercise the seams already in the model; they are a defender/validation practice, not an offensive trust violation.
The red-team deliverable phase (findings, CVSS, ATT&CK mapping, retest). Operator-side; no victim trust relationship to violate.

TA0011.reason: Active Directory has no command-and-control channel of its own; C2 rides the host/network. Permanent honest-na.

TA0010.reason: AD is not an egress; exfiltration leaves via host/network, not the directory service. Permanent.

TA0011.reason: A passive retrieval index does not beacon. C2 that abuses it is modeled as memory-as-dead-drop on the agent, not the store. Permanent.

TA0006.reason: A file conduit holds no credentials to access; credential access targets stores, not the conduit. Permanent.
